content.text:
Lets start with the assets and data lineage piece. How you want to do it?
you can be orchestrator and deligate the. task to opus/sonet/haiku.

Good starting point — before I propose an approach, let me pull together what "assets & lineage" already means in this repo (issue #596, the dbt artifact work #609/#611, and the current data model), fanning that out to parallel agents while I check the GitHub issues directly.

input.prompt:
In the repo /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ, gather everything documented about "assets" and "data lineage" as a planned feature. Search breadth: very thorough.

Look at:
- context/post-v1-roadmap.md — any sections on lineage, assets, incident management, G-d gap, issue #596, dbt manifest/lineage
- docs/progress.md — the v1.1 cycle plan (which week is lineage/#596 scheduled, what the exit gates say)
- docs/adr/README.md + any ADRs touching dbt (0029), Iceberg (0030), or lineage/asset concepts
- docs/ design docs (any *.md mentioning lineage, asset, catalog, impact analysis)

Return a structured summary: (1) what the project has already committed to or sketched for assets/lineage (verbatim key excerpts with file:line refs), (2) which GitHub issues are referenced, (3) what inputs exist (e.g. dbt manifest.json artifacts in ADLS), (4) any explicit scope decisions or anti-goals. Be precise — this feeds a design decision.

input.description: Survey backend model for lineage fit
input.prompt:
In /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend, survey the current backend architecture with an eye to adding an "assets + data lineage" layer. Search breadth: thorough.

Specifically:
1. app/db/models — list ALL SQLAlchemy models/tables with their key columns (suites, checks, runs, results, connections, pipeline_runs, trigger_bindings, schedules, anything else). How is a check's target table/dataset identified today (Suite.target? run_target? per-check table)? Exact shape of that target field.
2. app/orchestration/ — the dbt provider (#609/#611, ADR 0029): does it already parse run_results.json and/or manifest.json? What does it store into pipeline_runs? Does any lineage-ish data (node names, depends_on) already flow in? File:line refs.
3. app/datasources/ — how connections identify databases/schemas/tables (the introspection/catalog endpoints used by the check editor, column profiler) — is there any existing "list tables" surface that could seed an asset inventory?
4. app/api/ — router inventory (one line each) so we know where a new /assets or /lineage router would sit; and how authz scoping works (owned-or-shared suites, workspace admin).
5. Alembic: how many migrations, latest revision id.

Return a compact structured report with file:line refs. Do not paste whole files.

gh issue list --search "lineage OR asset in:title" --state all --json number,title,state,milestone --limit 20 -q '.[] | "\(.number) [\(.state)] \(.milestone.title // "no-ms") — \(.title)"' 2>&1; echo "---"; gh issue view 611 --json title,state,body -q '"\(.title) [\(.state)]\n\(.body)"' 2>&1 | head -60

596 [OPEN] v1.1 Week 6 — scale-aware execution + hardening + cycle close — Incident & lineage design doc (gap G-d) — incident objects, asset anchoring, lineage pull
717 [OPEN] v1.1 Backlog — Revisit Iceberg v3 support (deletion vectors, row lineage) behind a capability gate — ADR 0030
742 [OPEN] v1.1 Backlog — feat(rbac): in-app role management — PATCH role endpoint, last-admin guard, audit line, Admin UI editor (ADR 0033)
310 [OPEN] v1.1 Week 6 — scale-aware execution + hardening + cycle close — decision (ADR): history/audit strategy — check-version history lost on delete, no audit log, no soft-delete
609 [CLOSED] v1.1 Week 2 — Portability: OTel logs, secrets lifecycle, dry-run depth — dbt-modeled lineage objects on Snowflake (views + dynamic tables) via self-hosted dbt Core
597 [OPEN] v1.1 Week 6 — scale-aware execution + hardening + cycle close — Epic: v1.1 cycle (Weeks 1–6, 2026-07-04 → 2026-08-15)
286 [CLOSED] v1.1 Week 2 — Portability: OTel logs, secrets lifecycle, dry-run depth — Post-v1: Apache Iceberg v2 / v3 table format support
611 [CLOSED] v1.1 Week 2 — Portability: OTel logs, secrets lifecycle, dry-run depth — dbt as a third OrchestrationProvider (webhook + artifact-poll, host-agnostic)
716 [CLOSED] v1.1 Week 3 — Azure wind-down + local-first posture — Implement native Iceberg read: `IcebergCheckRunner` + self-contained `iceberg` connection (ADR 0030)
---
dbt as a third OrchestrationProvider (webhook + artifact-poll, host-agnostic) [CLOSED]
Split from #609 (user request 2026-07-04): exercise dbt as a **third orchestration layer** — a real second-generation test of the `OrchestrationProvider` seam (ADR 0004/0011), not dbt clubbed under the already-proven Airflow provider.

**Design principle — bind to dbt's universal surface, not a host API.** dbt Core has no runs API; what every dbt deployment produces, regardless of where it runs, is **artifacts** (`run_results.json`, `manifest.json`) and the ability to run a command after a build. So the provider contract mirrors the Airflow one exactly, host-agnostically:

1. **Push (near-real-time):** a thin wrapper / `on-run-end` hook POSTs an HMAC-signed event to `POST /api/v1/orchestration/events/dbt` (same pattern + signing scheme as the Airflow callback, ADR 0007; snippet ships in `integrations/dbt/` like `integrations/airflow/`).
2. **Poll fallback (10-min beat):** the provider reads `run_results.json` from a configured artifacts location (ADLS/S3 blob path now; local volume post-wind-down) — same cadence + gap-recovery shape as ADF/Airflow polling.

**Mapping:** `provider='dbt'`, `pipeline_or_dag_id` = dbt project (or job) name, `provider_run_id` = dbt `invocation_id`; runs land in `pipeline_runs`; `trigger_bindings` works unchanged (provider-agnostic composite key). Failure events alert; success events trigger bound suites — e.g. rebuild of the #609 dynamic tables triggers the DataQ suite on the mart.

**Scope (in-repo feature work):**
- [ ] `DbtProvider` implementing `OrchestrationProvider` (no provider-specific branching outside it)
- [ ] `dbt` orchestration connection type behind `ConnectionAdapter` (artifacts location + webhook signing key in SecretStore) — orchestration category, NOT a datasource (CLAUDE.md §4)
- [ ] `POST /orchestration/events/dbt` receiver (HMAC, uniform-401, replay posture per ADR 0007)
- [ ] `integrations/dbt/` callback snippet + README
- [ ] Poll fallback + gap recovery on the existing 10-min beat
- [ ] Tests: provider unit + receiver auth battery (adversarial) + poll parsing of real `run_results.json` fixtures
- [ ] Live verify against the #609 project in the Azure window; trigger-on-success fires a suite run

**Why this is the neutral choice** (vs vendor hostings — full comparison on #609): dbt Cloud free tier has no API/scheduler; dbt Projects on Snowflake observes via Snowflake and dies with the trial; Databricks dbt tasks observe via the Jobs API. The artifact/webhook contract works under ALL of those hosts and under local compose — neutrality by construction (ADR 0010/0013).

Depends on #609 (needs a dbt project producing runs). ADR required (dbt provider model — sibling to ADR 0006/0007).

content.text:
Both survey agents are still working. Quick status of what I've confirmed so far from GitHub:

- **#596** (v1.1 W6) is the committed vehicle: an *incident & lineage design doc* — incident lifecycle, an **asset entity** (OpenLineage dataset naming) as the anchor, a "pull-don't-build" lineage slice, ownership routing, and RCA-card-as-payload — plus filing the phase-1 build issues (asset-entity migration first).
- **#609/#611 are closed**: a real dbt project (RETAIL → staging views → mart dynamic tables) already runs against Snowflake, and the `DbtProvider` already polls its artifacts (`run_results.json`, with `manifest.json` available in ADLS) — that's a ready-made lineage source to design against.

Once the two agents report back with the roadmap themes (3/14/2/5) and the current DB-model shape, I'll come back with a concrete "how I'd do it" plan including which pieces I'd delegate to which model tier.

<result># Assets & Data Lineage — Everything Documented (as of 2026-07-10)

## 1. What the project has already committed to / sketched

### Origin: the pre-v1 sketch (superseded, kept for lineage of the idea)
`context/DataQ_platform_roadmap.md:392-393`:
> **Theme G — Lineage, engines & sources**
> - Table / asset lineage — visual dependency graph (ADF / Airflow → tables / files → suites → checks), React Flow; uses data already in connections + suite config; ~1 week

This was a one-line, effort-estimated ("~1 week") sketch from the original v1 roadmap. It has since been superseded by a much more deliberate treatment in `context/post-v1-roadmap.md` (below) — the "~1 week, build our own graph" idea is explicitly walked back in favor of pulling lineage from external catalogs/OpenLineage.

### The core gap statement — G-d
`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:63`:
> | G-d | **No incident workflow, no lineage, no ownership routing** — runs + alerts exist; "what broke downstream / who owns it / when was it resolved" doesn't. No data-access audit trail (the HIPAA gate). | This is what DQ products are bought for | **Theme 9** (results/reporting depth) + **Theme 4** / #431 (audit trail); lineage/incident objects = new design doc needed; the incident-*narrative* half is design-captured as **Theme 2's agentic root-cause analysis** (2026-07-04); lineage may be *pulled/emitted* rather than built — **Theme 14** governance-catalog + OpenLineage capture (2026-07-04); incident objects anchor to the **asset entity** (Theme 3's Asset-first IA capture, phase 1 = the shared prerequisite) |

`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:70-74` (prioritization signal):
> G-a and G-b are the two that change what the product *is* (coverage-without-authoring + scale-safe execution); **G-d is what makes it *usable in anger* (incidents/lineage)**; the rest are adoption/positioning. Recommended post-v1 opening sequence: Theme 1 `schema_drift` + `anomaly` … → scale-aware execution (G-b, Theme 7) → **incident/lineage design doc (G-d)**.

### Theme 3 — Asset-first IA (the "asset" concept), design-captured 2026-07-04
`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:205-244` — full section "Asset-first IA (design-captured 2026-07-04 — the lineage-era navigation model)":

- **The inversion, deliberately partial** (`:207-215`):
  > When lineage lands (Theme 14), invert the navigation from suite-first to asset-first — but split the noun from the verb rather than replacing one with the other. Assets become what users *browse and reason about* (health, lineage, incidents, blast radius); suites remain how checks *execute* (batching, scheduling, trigger bindings, notifications, sharing). Making assets primary for *everything* would fight three structural facts: authz is suite-scoped (ADR 0027) …; scheduling / triggers / notifications are genuinely batch-level operational concepts; and GX itself is suite-shaped, so the engine grain stays suites regardless. The products that do this well (dbt models-vs-jobs, Monte Carlo assets-vs-monitors) all run this two-axis model.

- **The enabling primitive** (`:217-227`):
  > a first-class **asset entity**. Today "the table" exists only implicitly inside `Suite.target` and check configs. Lineage needs asset *nodes*; the asset page needs asset *identity*; G-d incidents need something to *attach to*; governance-catalog sync (Theme 14) needs an entity to *map to*. One migration serves all four — schedule it before any of them.
  >
  > **Asset identity = the OpenLineage dataset naming spec (namespace + name), adopted as the canonical key from day one.** It answers the hardest resolution problems (the same logical table via DEV vs QA connections, flat-file patterns as assets, UC three-level names) with a vendor-neutral convention, and makes the Theme-14 OpenLineage emission/pull interop automatic instead of a mapping layer.

- **Four shippable phases** (`:229-241`):
  1. **Asset entity + resolution** — `assets` table keyed by OpenLineage naming + mapping of checks/runs to asset IDs. "The migration everything else rides."
  2. **Asset view** — read-only aggregation across suites, with authz filtered to caller's suite grants (404-no-leak discipline).
  3. **Lineage attach** — asset pages grow upstream/downstream + blast radius; the Theme-2 RCA dossier and G-d incident objects anchor to assets.
  4. **Navigation inversion** — sidebar leads with Assets, suites demote to "execution groups." *"This is the step where DataQ stops feeling like 'GX-as-a-service' and starts feeling like a data-observability product."*

- **Status:** `:243-244` — "No issues filed yet — phase 1 files first when picked up (it's the dependency of the other three and of Theme 14's governance sync)."

- Also referenced from the Theme 3 table (`:168`):
  > | _(no issue yet)_ | **Data-asset-centric view** — the biggest IA gap (added 2026-07-04): the UI is suite-centric but users think table-centric ("is `orders` healthy?"). A dataset page aggregating every check, result, freshness state, and trend across all suites targeting that table/file pattern. … **Phase 2 of the Asset-first IA capture below** — build the asset entity first.

### Theme 14 — Data-governance catalogs (+ OpenLineage) — the lineage sourcing decision
`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:518-537`:

> Two directions, one seam:
> - **Pull** — consume the catalog's glossary/PII classifications as an authoritative classification source (feeds Theme 4's privacy pack + #433, ranked above profiler heuristics), and **pull lineage** to power the RCA blast-radius (Theme 2) and gap G-d **instead of building a lineage graph ourselves.**
> - **Push** — publish DQ results as **quality facets/assertions on catalog entities**, so data consumers see check status where they shop for data. DataHub and OpenMetadata both have first-class assertion/data-quality APIs — natural open-source reference impls; Microsoft Purview covers the Azure story; Collibra / Atlan / Alation are commercial targets behind the same seam.
> - **Open standard first: emit OpenLineage events** from runs — one vendor-neutral event stream consumed by Marquez, DataHub, Purview, Atlan and the orchestrators' own lineage backends; cheaper and more neutral than any point integration, and the likely first slice.
>
> **Shared primitive:** this whole section keys on the **asset entity** (Theme 3's Asset-first IA capture, phase 1) with **OpenLineage dataset naming as the canonical identity** — build that first; catalog mapping, lineage nodes, and quality-facet publishing all resolve through it.

**This is the single most important scope decision:** DataQ plans to **pull/emit lineage via OpenLineage and external catalogs (Marquez/DataHub/Purview/Atlan/OpenMetadata)**, not build its own lineage graph engine.

### Incident objects (paired concept)
`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:501-509` (Theme 14, Incident/ITSM tier 2):
> Tier 1 (create-only PagerDuty/Opsgenie/ServiceNow/Jira …) lives in Theme 5 … Tier 2 is the ITSM-grade half: **DataQ incident objects (the G-d design)** sync *both ways* … and the **Theme 2 agentic-RCA evidence card is the incident payload**, so the ticket arrives with the diagnosis pre-attached. Per-vendor impls behind one `IncidentProvider`-shaped seam, only after tier 1 proves demand.

Also Theme 2 agentic RCA (`:150-153`): the RCA dossier "compounds with Theme 1 (anomaly baselines sharpen the dossier)" and is explicitly called "the incident-narrative half of gap G-d."

## 2. GitHub issues referenced

| Issue | What it is | Status as of 2026-07-10 |
|---|---|---|
| **#596** | **Incident & lineage design doc** — "incident objects, asset-entity anchoring, OpenLineage-first pull → files next cycle's phase-1 issues." Scheduled **v1.1 Week 6** (`docs/progress.md:250`). | ⬜ not started |
| **#609** | "dbt-modeled lineage objects on Snowflake — G-d test bed"; **rescoped 2026-07-04 to self-hosted dbt Core** (not Airflow-hosted). Renamed in practice to "Self-hosted dbt Core lineage on Snowflake" (RETAIL→ANALYTICS_STG views→ANALYTICS dynamic tables). Unblocks #611. | ✅ **Closed 2026-07-05** (`docs/progress.md:160,121-126`) |
| **#611** | dbt as a **third `OrchestrationProvider`** (split from #609) — ADR 0029. Webhook + artifact-poll, host-agnostic. | ✅ **Closed 2026-07-05** — deployed to prod, webhook live-verified |
| **#431** | Compliance G1: data-access audit trail (the HIPAA gate) — tied to G-d in the gap table. | ⬜ W7 stretch |
| **#310** | History/audit strategy ADR (audit log + soft-delete decision) — "pairs with the **#596 design doc** (mapped 2026-07-04)." Scheduled W6 alongside #596. | ⬜ not started |
| **#461** | PATs — cited as the hard dependency enabling checks-as-code / CI gate work in Theme 14, tangential to lineage but part of the same theme. | ✅ Closed |
| (no issue yet) | Asset entity / asset-first IA phase 1 — explicitly "no issue yet," to be filed by the #596 design doc. | design-only |
| (no issue yet) | OpenLineage emission, catalog pull/push integrations (DataHub/OpenMetadata/Purview) | design-only |

**Cycle framing** (`docs/progress.md:26,111-113`): the v1.1 cycle's Weeks 4–6 run "the roadmap's recommended opening sequence (Theme-1 `schema_drift` + `anomaly` → scale-aware execution G-b → **incident/lineage design G-d**)."

## 3. Which week is #596 scheduled, and exit-gate language

`docs/progress.md` — **v1.1 Week 6** (due 2026-08-15), row `:250`:
> | ⬜ | [#596] Incident & lineage **design doc** (incident objects, asset-entity anchoring, OpenLineage-first pull) → files next cycle's phase-1 issues | G-d |

Adjacent row `:260`:
> | ⬜ | [#310] History/audit strategy ADR (audit log + soft-delete decision) — pairs with the #596 design doc (mapped 2026-07-04) | Theme 6 / ADR 0020 |

**W6 Exit gate** (`docs/progress.md:263-266`):
> a deliberately oversized local file / UC table runs under the memory cap with sampled-ness recorded (vs the #587 baseline); perf + hardening/small-bug batches green; **the G-d design doc (#596) + audit ADR (#310) merged with next-cycle phase-1 issues filed**; retro written; `v1.1.0` tagged.

**"Not scheduled" / next-cycle generator input** (`docs/progress.md:290-298`):
> What remains unscheduled is the design-captured, not-yet-filed work themed in post-v1-roadmap.md: Theme 2 LLM authoring/RCA … **Theme 3 asset entity + asset-first IA (phase 1 gets filed by the #596 design doc)**, the Theme 8 adapter expansion …, Theme 14 ecosystem integrations, and the Theme 3/4 privacy/a11y packs. These are the **v1.2 generator input**.

So: **#596 does not build assets/lineage itself — it is a design doc whose output is the issue list that will build phase-1 asset entity + lineage attach, filed as v1.2 work.**

## 4. Inputs that already exist (the lineage "test bed")

Confirmed by ADR 0029 (`docs/adr/0029-dbt-orchestration-provider.md:12`):
> What *every* dbt deployment produces, wherever it runs, is **artifacts** (`run_results.json`, `manifest.json`) and the ability to run a command after a build.

But — important nuance — **only `run_results.json` is consumed today**, for orchestration/trigger purposes, not lineage:
- ADR 0029, `:35-37`: `list_recent_runs` reads `<artifacts_uri>/<job>/latest/run_results.json` — used purely to detect success/failure and fire trigger bindings.
- `docs/orchestration.md:36-52` and `integrations/dbt/README.md` — same: webhook + poll of `run_results.json` only. **No code currently parses `manifest.json`** (which is where dbt's model dependency graph / lineage edges actually live).
- The artifact store already wired: **ADLS (`adls://`), S3 (`s3://`), or local (`file://`)**, via the existing ADLS/S3 auth shapes (`backend/app/datasources/adls.py`, `s3.py` reused as readers). Confirmed live-verified on Snowflake+ADLS (`docs/progress.md:160-161`, #609/#611 closed 2026-07-05, "artifacts to ADLS").
- So the raw input (**dbt `manifest.json` in ADLS**, produced by the same self-hosted dbt Core project from #609) already exists in the harness and is reachable through the same 3-scheme artifact reader built for #611 — but nothing yet reads it for lineage; that's squarely #596/phase-3 ("Lineage attach") scope.

Also noted as a Theme-14 "asset identity" input: **OpenLineage dataset naming spec (namespace + name)** is the chosen canonical key (`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:223-227`) — not dbt-specific, meant to also resolve UC three-level names, DEV/QA connection duplicates, and flat-file patterns.

## 5. Explicit scope decisions / anti-goals

1. **Do not build a proprietary lineage graph engine.** `context/post-v1-roadmap.md:524-525`: pull lineage from governance catalogs / OpenLineage "**instead of building a lineage graph ourselves**." This directly supersedes the earlier `DataQ_platform_roadmap.md:393` sketch ("visual dependency graph … React Flow … ~1 week"), which is no longer the plan.
2. **Assets are a separate concept from suites, not a replacement.** Suites keep owning execution (scheduling, triggers, notifications, authz); assets own browsing/health/lineage/incidents/blast-radius (`context/post-v1-roadmap.md:207-215`). Explicitly rejected: "making assets primary for *everything*" — cited reasons: suite-scoped authz (ADR 0027) re-keying cost, scheduling/triggers being batch-level concepts, and GX's suite-shaped execution grain.
3. **Asset identity is the OpenLineage naming spec, not an internal ID scheme** — chosen specifically to make future OpenLineage emission/pull "automatic instead of a mapping layer" (`:223-227`).
4. **dbt orchestration seam deliberately does not couple to any host API** (Snowflake tasks, Databricks Jobs, dbt Cloud API) — ADR 0029 alternatives-considered section (`docs/adr/0029:61-65`): "dbt under the Airflow provider — rejected," "Bind to a host API — rejected: each couples observation to one vendor, violating ADR 0010/0013." This keeps the artifact-based (and eventually lineage-capable) surface vendor-neutral.
5. **Sequencing anti-goal: incidents/lineage is explicitly not being built before its prerequisite.** "One migration serves all four [lineage, asset view, incidents, catalog sync] — schedule it before any of them" — i.e., no lineage attach work should start before the asset entity phase 1 lands (`:220-221`).
6. **#596 itself is scoped as a design doc only**, producing next-cycle issues — not an implementation task in v1.1. Confirmed by both the roadmap doc and progress.md exit-gate wording ("design doc … merged with next-cycle phase-1 issues filed").
7. No ADR yet exists dedicated to lineage/assets (checked `docs/adr/README.md` in full — only ADR 0029 dbt-provider and ADR 0030 Iceberg touch adjacent ground; ADR 0030 only tangentially connects by noting native Iceberg snapshot/schema history is "the natural feed for the reserved `freshness`/`schema_drift` monitor kinds," not lineage). The asset/lineage ADR is expected to originate from #596's output.

## Summary for the design decision

- **Committed direction:** build a first-class `assets` table (OpenLineage namespace+name identity) as phase 1, keep suites for execution, and **pull/emit lineage via OpenLineage rather than building an in-house graph**, catalog vendors (DataHub/OpenMetadata/Purview/Atlan) as pull/push targets.
- **Immediate blocker/vehicle:** issue **#596** (v1.1 Week 6, due 2026-08-15) is a *design doc*, paired with **#310** (audit/history ADR), whose deliverable is the filed phase-1 issue set (asset entity, then asset view, then lineage attach, then nav inversion).
- **Available data plumbing today:** dbt artifacts (`run_results.json` consumed; `manifest.json` present but unconsumed) sitting in ADLS/S3/local via the #611 `DbtProvider`'s 3-scheme artifact reader — the natural, already-live input for a future lineage-parsing step, but not yet wired to anything.
- **Nothing is implemented yet** for assets or lineage beyond the self-hosted dbt Core test bed (#609) and the orchestration provider (#611) that observes its *runs* (not its *dependency graph*).</result>

The roadmap survey is in — the key facts: the project has already decided **pull-don't-build** lineage (OpenLineage identity, no in-house graph engine), the **asset entity migration is the prerequisite for everything** (lineage, incidents, asset pages, catalog sync), and #596 is scoped as a design doc that files the phase-1 build issues. The dbt `manifest.json` (the actual dependency graph) already lands in ADLS but nothing reads it yet.

Still waiting on the backend data-model survey; I'll deliver the full proposal when it lands.

# Backend Survey: Assets + Data Lineage Readiness

## 1. `app/db/models.py` — full model inventory

Constants (models.py:26-82): `CONNECTION_TYPES` = snowflake, adls_gen2, s3, unity_catalog, iceberg, adf, airflow, dbt; `CHECK_KINDS` = expectation, freshness, volume, schema_drift, anomaly, comparison; `ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS` = adf, airflow, dbt; `PERMISSIONS` = view, edit, admin; `ENVS` = dev, qa, uat, prod.

| Model | Table | Key columns | Ref |
|---|---|---|---|
| `User` | users | id, aad_object_id, email, display_name | models.py:111-120 |
| `ApiKey` | api_keys | user_id (FK), key_prefix, key_hash, expires_at, revoked_at | models.py:123-149 |
| `Connection` | connections | id, name, **type** (str, one of CONNECTION_TYPES incl. orchestration providers), env, **config** (JSONB), secret_ref (SecretStore pointer), created_by; unique (name, env); partial-unique (type, env) for orchestration singletons | models.py:152-195 |
| `ConnectionVersion` | connection_versions | connection_id, version_no, name/type/env/config snapshot, changed_by | models.py:198-248 |
| `Suite` | suites | id, name, connection_id (FK), **target: JSONB\|None** — see below, column_policy (JSONB), created_by | models.py:251-288 |
| `Check` | checks | id, suite_id (FK, CASCADE), name, kind, expectation_type, warn/fail/critical_threshold, config (JSONB), alert_snoozed_until | models.py:291-324 |
| `CheckVersion` | check_versions | check_id, version_no, name/kind/expectation_type/config snapshot, changed_by | models.py:327-373 |
| `Run` | runs | id, suite_id (FK CASCADE), status, triggered_by (e.g. `dbt:<pipeline>:<run_id>` marker), celery_task_id, started/finished_at, failure_reason; partial-unique on (suite_id, triggered_by) for orchestration markers | models.py:376-418 |
| `Result` | results | id, run_id (FK), check_id (FK), status, metric_value, duration_ms, observed_value/expected_value (JSONB), sample_failures (JSONB) + purge timestamp | models.py:421-449 |
| `Share` | shares | suite_id, user_id, permission (view/edit/admin); unique (suite_id,user_id) | models.py:452-476 |
| `PipelineRun` | pipeline_runs | id, provider (adf/airflow/dbt), connection_id, provider_run_id, **pipeline_or_dag_id**, env, status, started/finished_at, failure_reason, last_updated_at; unique (provider, provider_run_id) | models.py:479-502 |
| `TriggerBinding` | trigger_bindings | provider, pipeline_or_dag_id, env, suite_id (FK CASCADE), enabled; unique (provider, pipeline_or_dag_id, env, suite_id) | models.py:505-535 |
| `Schedule` | schedules | suite_id (FK CASCADE), cron, timezone, enabled, next_run_at (precomputed), last_run_at, created_by | models.py:538-576 |
| `SuiteNotification` | suite_notifications | suite_id (unique), enabled, alert_on, webhook/slack_webhook secret_refs, email_recipients | models.py:579-623ish |

**How a check's target dataset is identified today (models.py:264-269):**
`Suite.target: JSONB | None` — one field, **at the suite level, not per-check** (a suite targets exactly one table/dataset; all its checks run against that same target). Comment explicitly: *"Datasource-shaped run target (#215): the table / flat-file path / Unity Catalog 3-level name... Shaped like the column profiler request (`table`/`schema`/`catalog`/`path`/`file_format`)"*. `NULL` = targetless/not-runnable.

Resolution logic lives in `app/services/run_target.py:82` `resolve_target(conn_type, target) -> ResolvedTarget`:
- flat-file types (adls_gen2/s3): `path` (literal) or `pattern` (batch), mutually exclusive — run_target.py:95-110
- `snowflake`: requires `table`, optional `schema` — run_target.py:112-114
- `unity_catalog`: requires `table` **and** `catalog`, optional `schema` — run_target.py:116-121

So today there is no dedicated "asset" entity — the target is inline JSONB on `Suite`, one target per suite, resolved per-connection-type at run time. No table/schema/catalog is a first-class row anywhere.

## 2. `app/orchestration/` — dbt provider (ADR 0029, #609/#611)

`app/orchestration/dbt.py` (319 lines). Confirmed via full read + repo-wide grep for `manifest.json`/`depends_on`:

- **Only reads `run_results.json`** (`_RUN_RESULTS_RELPATH = "latest/run_results.json"`, dbt.py:37, dbt.py:103). **`manifest.json` is never referenced anywhere in the codebase** (grep across `app/orchestration/` and `app/services/orchestration_service.py` returned zero hits).
- `_status_from_results()` (dbt.py:185-190) iterates `run_results.json`'s `results[]` array purely to derive one aggregate pass/fail (`_DBT_FAILURE_STATUSES` = error/fail/runtime error) — **per-node status is read but immediately collapsed to a single overall status; individual node names/results are discarded**, not persisted.
- `list_recent_runs()` (dbt.py:278-319) builds a `RunUpdate` per job from `doc["metadata"]` (`invocation_id`, `generated_at`, `invocation_started_at`) and `doc.get("results", [])` — no `depends_on`, no model/test node names, no `nodes`/`manifest` keys are extracted.
- `parse_event()` (webhook path, dbt.py:230-269) consumes DataQ's own callback shape (`project_name`, `job_name`, `invocation_id`, `status`, `started_at`, `finished_at`, `error`) — also no lineage fields.
- Both paths normalize to `RunUpdate` (orchestration/base.py:37-54): `provider_run_id, pipeline_or_dag_id, resource_name, status, started_at, finished_at, failure_reason` — this dataclass is the **only** shape that reaches persistence (`services/orchestration_service.py`, docstring at base.py:14-16), and it maps 1:1 onto `PipelineRun` columns (models.py:479-502). No node names, no `depends_on`, no lineage graph data flows in or is stored anywhere today.

**Conclusion:** the dbt provider is status/timing-only. Adding lineage would require a new artifact reader for `manifest.json` (node ids, `depends_on.nodes`, resource_type, database/schema/alias per node) — no scaffolding for this exists yet, but the `_read_artifact()` scheme-dispatch helper (dbt.py:102-173, handles file/adls/s3) could be reused/generalized to also fetch `manifest.json` from the same `artifacts_uri`.

## 3. `app/datasources/` — connection introspection / catalog surface

- `ConnectionAdapter` protocol (`app/datasources/base.py:128-146`) only exposes `validate_config` and `test` (connectivity probe) — **no `list_tables`/`list_schemas`/`list_databases` method** in the interface at all.
- `CheckRunner`/`MonitorRunner` protocols (base.py:93-126) only run checks/monitors against an already-known target — no catalog browsing.
- The only "listing" surface in the whole backend is in `app/services/profile_service.py`, and it requires the table/path to already be known (used by the column-profiler / check-editor, not an inventory browser):
  - `list_columns()` (profile_service.py:969-1000) — dispatches to `list_table_columns` (SQL/Iceberg, needs `table`+`schema`/`catalog`) or `list_file_columns` (flat-file, needs `path`). Backing helpers: `list_table_columns` (profile_service.py:896), `list_iceberg_columns` (profile_service.py:781), `list_file_columns` (profile_service.py:930).
  - Exposed via `suites.py`: `GET .../columns` (suites.py:445, gated 'edit') and `POST /suites/{suite_id}/profile` (suites.py:387-404).
  - `suggest_policy_for_target()` (profile_service.py:1004) derives the column_policy suggestion the same way.
- **No "list tables in this connection/schema" endpoint exists anywhere** — you must already know the fully-qualified table name to profile/list its columns. This means an asset inventory has **no existing seed surface**; it would need a new introspection method per datasource adapter (Snowflake `INFORMATION_SCHEMA`, Unity Catalog catalog/schema/table listing API, S3/ADLS prefix listing, Iceberg namespace listing) — `app/datasources/snowflake.py`, `unity_catalog.py`, `iceberg.py`, `s3.py`, `adls.py` currently only implement connect+run-checks, not catalog browsing.

## 4. `app/api/` — router inventory

All mounted in `app/main.py:184-198` under prefix `/api/v1`:

| Router file | Tag | One-liner |
|---|---|---|
| me.py | auth | current-user identity endpoint |
| api_keys.py | auth | PAT mint/list/revoke (ADR 0026) |
| users.py | users | user directory search (for share grants) |
| probe.py | probe | Week-1 exit-gate health probe |
| connections.py | connections | connection CRUD + test/reauth + version history |
| suites.py | suites | suite CRUD + column profiler + policy suggestion endpoints |
| checks.py | checks | check CRUD nested under a suite (GX expectations) |
| notifications.py | notifications | per-suite alert delivery config |
| runs.py | runs | run/result/pipeline-run read API |
| dashboard.py | dashboard | Enhanced Monitoring Dashboard summary (ADR 0022) |
| schedules.py | schedules | cron schedule CRUD (A7) |
| shares.py | shares | suite share grant/revoke |
| orchestration.py | orchestration | ADF/Airflow/dbt webhook receivers (machine-to-machine, HMAC/SP auth, no Azure AD user) |
| trigger_bindings.py | trigger-bindings | suite-run trigger binding CRUD |
| admin.py | (admin) | workspace-admin read endpoints (all-suites/all-users/access overview), gated by `require_workspace_admin` |

A new `/assets` or `/lineage` router would slot in analogously to `suites.py`/`connections.py` — same `include_router(..., prefix="/api/v1")` pattern in main.py.

**Authz scoping** (two orthogonal mechanisms):
- **Per-suite ladder** — `app/services/suite_authz.py`: `effective_permission()` (suite_authz.py:72-85) ranks `owner` (suite.created_by) > `admin` (implicit for workspace-admins, ADR 0027) > share row (`edit`>`view`) > None. Gate function `require_permission(session, suite_id, user_id, minimum=...)` (suite_authz.py:113-137) 404s if no access (hides existence), 403s if below minimum. Any endpoint on suite-scoped resources (checks, schedules, notifications) uses this.
- **Workspace-admin** — `app/core/auth.py:229-258`: `is_workspace_admin(user)` checks email against `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` config allowlist (config.py:169-185); `require_workspace_admin` is a FastAPI dependency (auth.py:244) used to gate `admin.py`'s workspace-wide endpoints (403, not 404 — existence isn't suite-scoped there).
- For a new assets/lineage layer: if assets are connection-scoped (not suite-owned), likely needs its own authz rule (e.g., visible to anyone who can see the connection, or gated to workspace-admin) rather than reusing `suite_authz` directly, since connections have no owner/share model today (only `created_by` on `Connection`, no `ConnectionShare`).

## 5. Alembic

- **21 migration files** in `alembic/versions/` (confirmed via linear revision-chain walk: exactly one file has no other migration pointing `down_revision` at it → single head, no branching).
- **Latest head revision: `e716a1b2c3d4`** — `alembic/versions/e716a1b2c3d4_widen_connection_type_for_iceberg.py:35-36` (down_revision = `c605d1e2f3a4`), message: "widen connection type-set for the native iceberg datasource".</result>

Both surveys are back — here's the full picture and how I'd run it.

## What we're building on

**Already decided (roadmap + #596):** lineage is **pull-don't-build** — no in-house graph engine. The plan of record is: a first-class **asset entity** keyed by the OpenLineage dataset-naming spec (namespace + name) as phase 1, then an asset health view, then lineage attach (blast radius), then the nav inversion. Incidents anchor to assets. #596 (currently v1.1 W6) is scoped as the *design doc* that pins all this down and files the phase-1 build issues; it pairs with the #310 audit/history ADR.

**What exists today:**
- No asset concept anywhere — a suite's target is one inline JSONB blob ([models.py:264](backend/app/db/models.py#L264)), resolved per-connection-type in [run_target.py](backend/app/services/run_target.py).
- The dbt provider (#611) reads only `run_results.json` for status; **`manifest.json` — the actual dependency graph — already lands in ADLS every build but nothing consumes it.** The scheme-dispatch artifact reader (file/adls/s3) in [dbt.py:102](backend/app/orchestration/dbt.py#L102) is reusable for it.
- No list-tables introspection surface, and connections have no share model — asset authz will have to derive from suite grants (phase-2 design point).

## How I'd do it — two stages, orchestrated

**Stage 1 — the design doc + ADR (#596 pulled forward), ~1 PR.** This is the committed deliverable and everything else legally depends on it ("schedule the asset migration before any of them"). Delegation:

| Work | Who |
|---|---|
| Research: OpenLineage naming spec mapped to our 5 datasource types (Snowflake/UC/ADLS/S3/Iceberg — the DEV-vs-QA and flat-file-pattern identity problems), dbt `manifest.json` → OpenLineage name mapping, Marquez/DataHub/OpenMetadata pull-API comparison | 2–3 parallel **Sonnet** agents (docs/web research) |
| The design doc itself: asset entity schema, resolution rules from `Suite.target`, incident lifecycle + dedup-vs-alerts, ownership routing, RCA-card-as-payload, the first lineage pull slice | **Opus** (or I draft it myself) — the thinking-heavy piece |
| Phase-1 issue drafts, ADR-index/progress.md bookkeeping | **Haiku** |
| Synthesis, consistency with ADR 0027/0029/0033, PR assembly + `/code-review` | me (orchestrator) |

**Stage 2 — phase-1 build (separate PRs, after the doc merges):** the `assets` table migration + resolver (`Suite.target` + connection → namespace/name), backfill mapping of existing suites/runs, a read-only `/assets` API scoped through suite grants, and — the first real lineage pull — a `manifest.json` reader in the dbt provider producing asset edges. Sonnet handles the well-specified implementation + tests, Opus the resolver/authz design points, me reviewing.

**One timing argument for doing Stage 2 soon:** the Azure window closes ~2026-07-25. The dbt manifest in ADLS + live Snowflake dynamic tables is the only real lineage test bed we have, so if we want the manifest→edges slice *live-verified* rather than fixture-only, it needs to land inside the window (the `file://` scheme keeps it working locally afterwards).

## My recommendation

Start with **Stage 1 now** — I'd kick off the research agents and have a reviewed design doc + ADR + filed phase-1 issues in one PR (retargeting #596's milestone since we're pulling it from W6 into the current week). Then, if you're happy with the design, immediately follow with the asset-entity migration and the dbt-manifest lineage slice as Stage 2 while the Azure window is still open.

Want me to proceed on that basis, or would you rather compress it — skip straight to design-doc + asset-entity build in one push?
yeah go ahead as planned

input.prompt:
Research the OpenLineage dataset naming specification for a design doc. Use web search/fetch (openlineage.io docs and the OpenLineage GitHub spec — the canonical file is spec/Naming.md in github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage).

Deliver a precise, citation-backed report answering:

1. The exact namespace + name construction rules for each of these dataset types:
   - Snowflake (namespace format incl. account identifier/host; name = db.schema.table?)
   - Databricks / Unity Catalog (what does the spec say; how do people name UC three-level catalog.schema.table assets)
   - Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 / abfss:// paths
   - AWS S3 (s3://bucket, path handling)
   - Apache Iceberg tables read natively via a catalog (REST catalog / warehouse) — what's the convention when there's no engine host
2. How the spec treats case sensitivity, quoting, and default schema/database omission.
3. Known ambiguities/gotchas: same logical table reached via different hosts (DEV vs QA account) — does the spec make these distinct datasets (I believe yes, namespace differs)? What do practitioners do to group "the same logical asset across environments"?
4. How file-pattern datasets (e.g. a directory of daily-partitioned CSVs, glob patterns) are conventionally named — dataset = the directory/prefix, partitions as facets?
5. The dbt↔OpenLineage integration's naming behavior (openlineage-dbt): what namespace/name does it emit for Snowflake models — so our own naming can match it byte-for-byte.

Format: per-datasource table of namespace template + name template + a concrete example using placeholder values, then the gotchas section. Cite URLs. Your final message is the report — return it in full.

Research dbt's manifest.json as a lineage source, for a DataQ design doc. Two parts:

PART A — manifest schema (web: docs.getdbt.com artifacts docs + schemas.getdbt.com):
1. Which manifest keys carry the dependency graph: `nodes`, `sources`, `parent_map`/`child_map`, per-node `depends_on.nodes`, and per-node physical identity (`database`, `schema`, `alias`/`name`, `relation_name`), `resource_type`, `unique_id` format.
2. Manifest schema versioning (v10/v11/v12...): which fields are stable across recent dbt-core versions (1.7→1.10), and the recommended minimal stable subset to parse so a reader doesn't break on upgrades. How to detect the schema version (`metadata.dbt_schema_version`).
3. How sources (raw tables) vs models vs tests appear, and how to derive TABLE-level lineage edges (source table → model table) from it. Ephemeral models (no physical table) — how to collapse them out of the edge list.
4. Rough size of a manifest.json for a small project (~10 models) and for large ones — any streaming/parsing concerns.

PART B — our concrete test bed (local filesystem):
Look in ~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/ for the dbt project (search for dbt_project.yml; likely under a dbt/ or dbt-lineage directory — it models RETAIL source tables → ANALYTICS_STG staging views → ANALYTICS mart dynamic tables on Snowflake). Read its dbt_project.yml, sources yml, and model files, and produce the EXPECTED lineage graph: list every source (database.schema.table) and model (database.schema.name, materialization) and the edges between them. Also note where its artifacts are uploaded (search for run_results/manifest upload scripting, ADLS path raw/dbt/latest).

Format: Part A as a concise reference with citations; Part B as an explicit node+edge list. Your final message is the report — return it in full.

Research report for a design doc: DataQ (a data-quality platform, FastAPI/Python 3.13, MIT-licensed, vendor-neutral by policy) plans to PULL lineage from external systems and EMIT OpenLineage events rather than build its own lineage graph. Compare the integration options (web research; cite URLs):

1. **OpenLineage emission (push from DataQ):** the openlineage-python client — license (must be Apache/MIT/BSD compatible; no GPL/AGPL/SSPL), transport options (HTTP, file, console), event model (RunEvent with job + inputs/outputs datasets), effort to emit "DQ suite run X read dataset Y" events, and the dataQuality/assertions facet (DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet) for publishing check results.
2. **Marquez:** lineage pull API (GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:...), deployment weight (Postgres + Java service), license, suitability as the local-first reference consumer.
3. **DataHub:** lineage read API (GraphQL/OpenAPI), assertion/data-quality push API, Python SDK license (acryl-datahub — check the license), OpenLineage ingestion support, deployment weight.
4. **OpenMetadata:** lineage API + data-quality/test-results push, Python SDK license, OpenLineage support, deployment weight.
5. **Microsoft Purview:** lineage read (Apache Atlas API), OpenLineage support status, note it's the Azure story only (DataQ is winding down Azure — lower priority).
6. Anything notable about consuming lineage FROM orchestrators directly (Airflow's OpenLineage provider, dbt's openlineage-dbt wrapper) as pull sources.

Conclude with a recommended "first slice" ranking for a small team: what to build first for (a) blast-radius lineage pull and (b) quality-facet publishing, given local-first posture (docker-compose reference stack) and license constraints. Your final message is the report — return it in full.

In /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ, gather the repo context needed to write a new design doc + ADR for "assets + lineage + incidents" (issue #596). Search breadth: thorough. Return excerpts with file:line refs.

1. **Sibling design docs' format:** issue #596 says the doc should be "a docs/ sibling of the admin-ui / dq-intelligence notes". Find those files (likely docs/design-admin-ui*.md, docs/dq-intelligence*.md or similar under docs/ or context/) — report their exact filenames, heading structure, and typical length so the new doc matches the house style.
2. **ADR conventions:** docs/adr/ — the template/structure of a recent ADR (e.g. 0029 or 0033: heading set, Status/Context/Decision/Consequences?), the next free ADR number, and how docs/adr/README.md index lines are formatted (copy 2-3 example lines verbatim).
3. **Theme 2 (agentic RCA / evidence card)** in context/post-v1-roadmap.md: extract the full description of the deterministic "evidence card" (layer 1, no LLM) — what fields/evidence it contains; incidents will use it as payload.
4. **Theme 5 (alerting/incident tier 1)** in context/post-v1-roadmap.md: the alert-vs-incident relationship, dedup design, PagerDuty/ITSM tier-1 description.
5. **ADR 0027 access tiers + ADR 0033 workspace roles:** one-paragraph summary each of the authz model (owner/admin/share ladder; Admin/Member/Viewer roles) — asset-view authz must compose with these.
6. **docs/progress.md current-week section:** the v1.1 Week 3 table (where a pulled-forward #596 row would land) — copy the table's row format and the W3 exit-gate text verbatim; also the W6 rows for #596/#310 verbatim.
7. **Issue-filing conventions:** any docs describing required labels/milestones for new issues (CONTRIBUTING or memory of 'every issue gets label(s) + milestone'); list the label set used on recent feature issues (run `gh label list` if needed, and `gh issue view 592 --json labels,milestone,body | head -40` as an example of an Acceptance-criteria checklist issue).

Be complete but compact — this feeds directly into doc drafting.

<result>I now have all the material needed. Here is the compiled repo context for drafting the design doc + ADR for issue #596 ("assets + lineage + incidents").

## 1. Sibling design docs — house style to match

Two files, both under `docs/` (not `context/`):

- `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/docs/post-v1-admin-ui-notes.md` — **78 lines**
- `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/docs/post-v1-dq-intelligence-notes.md` — **137 lines**

**Common heading structure** (docs/post-v1-admin-ui-notes.md:1-13, docs/post-v1-dq-intelligence-notes.md:1-14):
```
# Post-v1 notes — <Area> (deferred design)

> **Status: deferred to post-v1.** Captured so the design intent isn't lost. <why-not-v1
> paragraph, 3-5 sentences, cites market comparables / gap ID>
>
> Related issues: **#NNN** (...), milestoned _..._.

## <Section 1 — e.g. "v1 access model (what actually ships)">
- bullet list, **bold lead-in term:** description

## Post-v1: <sub-area> (→ #NNN)
- narrative bullets, some with **Intent:** / **Decision (per the strategic review):** callouts

## <numbered or thematic sections continue...>
## Guiding principle for the post-v1 pickup / ## Guiding principle
<closing paragraph tying it back to the v1 loop / anti-scope-creep framing>
```
The dq-intelligence doc instead uses `## The four themes` → `### 1. ...` `### 2. ...` (numbered sub-`###` themes each with **Feasibility: High/Med · Effort: S/M/L** header line), then `## Sequencing` (ASCII diagram), `## LLM integration model (proposed — not locked)`, `## Guiding principle`.

**Style notes to replicate:** heavy cross-linking to issue numbers and ADRs in prose (not just tables); bold **Decision (rejected/chosen)** callouts; a closing "Guiding principle" section; length **~80-140 lines**, terse but dense — this is the target length for the new design doc (e.g. `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` or similar, doc it should also get linked from `context/post-v1-roadmap.md` Theme 2/3/5/14 the way the other two are).

## 2. ADR conventions

`/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/docs/adr/README.md` defines the template (lines ~9-38):
- Filename: `NNNN-short-kebab-slug.md`
- Frontmatter: **Status** (Proposed/Accepted/Deprecated/`Superseded by ADR-NNNN`), **Date** (`YYYY-MM-DD`), **Deciders**, optional **Consulted**/**Supersedes**/**Superseded by**/**Amends**
- Sections: `## Context`, `## Decision`, `## Consequences`, `## Alternatives considered`, optional `## Related`

## 5. Authz summaries (ADR 0027 + ADR 0033)

**ADR 0027** (`docs/adr/0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md`): Redefines the per-suite ladder as `view < edit < admin < owner`, where `owner` is the immutable creator (exactly one per suite) and `admin` is no longer a grantable peer tier — instead it's computed implicitly for **workspace-admins** (governance/break-glass superuser, sees and can act on every suite: manage shares, delete, edit, run, read — including failing-sample data). Normal users can only be granted `edit`/`view`, eliminating the admin-revokes-admin escalation edge; workspace-admins additionally get workspace-wide visibility on Dashboard/Suites/Results, not just the read-only `/admin` page (superseding #411/#412). **Asset-view authz must compose with this**: an asset page aggregates across suites, so per ADR 0027's discipline it must still filter to the caller's suite grants and use 404-existence-hiding for unshared suites' data — it can't shortcut through a separate asset-level ACL.

**ADR 0033** (`docs/adr/0033-workspace-roles-rbac.md`): Adds a second, coarse axis — three stored `users.role` values (`admin | member | viewer`, server-default `member`) that compose with (never replace) the fine per-suite ladder: a Member with no share on a suite still can't see it. The normative capability matrix gates suite-creation/edit-share-eligibility at Member+, caps Viewers at `view` (belt-and-braces: rejected at grant time AND capped in `effective_permission`), and makes **connection mutations Admin-only** (closing a workspace-global hole where any authenticated user could delete/re-credential shared connections). `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` demotes to bootstrap/break-glass (`is_workspace_admin` = stored role OR allowlist). **Implication for the new doc**: any new asset-entity endpoints/roles must decide where they sit on this matrix — e.g. "who can see an asset" = the suite-ladder axis (unchanged), "who can manage asset metadata/ownership routing" is a new capability that should probably gate on `edit`/`admin` per composing-suite, analogous to how connection-mutation was added as a new Admin-only row rather than a new axis.

<result>## PART A — dbt `manifest.json` as a lineage source

### 1. Where the dependency graph lives

| Key | What it holds |
|---|---|
| `nodes` | Dict of all **models, tests, seeds, snapshots, analyses, operations** (`on-run-start`/`on-run-end` hooks land here too), keyed by `unique_id`. Each entry carries `resource_type`, `depends_on.nodes` (+ `depends_on.macros`), `database`, `schema`, `alias`, `name`, `relation_name`, `config.materialized`, `package_name`, `path`/`original_file_path`, `checksum`, `columns`, `description`. |
| `sources` | Dict of source-table defs, same identity shape (`database`, `schema`, `name`, `relation_name`) but no `depends_on` (sources are graph roots). |
| `parent_map` | `unique_id → [first-order parent unique_ids]` for every node (models, tests, sources-as-keys don't appear as keys since they have no parents, but they appear as *values*). |
| `child_map` | Inverse of `parent_map`: `unique_id → [first-order children]`. This is the fast way to walk a source or model **forward**. |
| `disabled` | Nodes marked `enabled: false` — excluded from `nodes`/`sources` but listed here; usually irrelevant for lineage. |
| `metadata.dbt_schema_version` | A URL, e.g. `https://schemas.getdbt.com/dbt/manifest/v12.json` — self-describing version pointer. |

`parent_map`/`child_map` are the cheapest way to build a graph without re-deriving it from every node's `depends_on.nodes`, since they're already flattened and include sources.

**`unique_id` format:** `<resource_type>.<package_name>.<name>` for models/sources (source ids are `source.<package>.<source_name>.<table_name>`, one dot-segment deeper), tests append a short hash suffix, e.g. `test.dataq_lineage.not_null_mart_order_revenue_order_number.7a14e08b69`. This is confirmed against our own project's manifest (below).

**Physical identity** — `database` + `schema` + `alias` (the actual object name after any `alias()`/config override) together give the physical relation; `relation_name` is dbt's own precomputed `database.schema.alias` string (quoted per adapter), so in practice **`relation_name` alone is the table-level lineage node key** and you don't need to reconstruct it from the three parts yourself — except for ephemeral models, where `relation_name` is `null` (see §3).

Source: [Manifest JSON — dbt Developer Hub](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json), [dbt JSON Schemas](https://schemas.getdbt.com/).

### 2. Schema versioning — verified against actual dbt-core source, not just docs

Docs pages were vague/contradictory on the exact version↔release mapping, so I pip-downloaded the actual `dbt-core` wheels for 1.5.0 → 1.10.0 and grepped the `@schema_version("manifest", N)` decorator on the `WritableManifest` class (source of truth — this literally sets what gets written):

| dbt-core version | manifest schema version |
|---|---|
| 1.5.x | v9 |
| 1.6.x | v10 |
| 1.7.x | v11 |
| **1.8.x – 1.11.x (confirmed 1.9/1.10/1.11 all ship the identical `dbt/artifacts/schemas/manifest/v12/manifest.py`)** | **v12** |

Our local test project's actual `target/manifest.json` (built with dbt-core 1.11.12) has `metadata.dbt_schema_version = "https://schemas.getdbt.com/dbt/manifest/v12.json"` — consistent.

**Practical takeaway:** manifest v12 has been stable across dbt-core 1.8 through at least 1.11 (3+ years of minor releases) — a DataQ reader targeting v12 is safe for "1.8→current" without churn. Detect the version by parsing the last path segment of `metadata.dbt_schema_version` (`.../manifest/v12.json` → `12`) and gate on it (warn/best-effort-parse below v10, since `parent_map`/`child_map`/`depends_on.nodes` shapes are the same back to early versions and are extremely unlikely to change — they're load-bearing for dbt's own `--select state:modified` and docs site).

**Recommended minimal stable subset to parse** (won't break across 1.7→1.11+):
- `metadata.dbt_schema_version`, `metadata.dbt_version` (for logging/diagnostics)
- `nodes[*].resource_type`, `.unique_id`, `.database`, `.schema`, `.alias`, `.relation_name`, `.config.materialized`, `.depends_on.nodes`
- `sources[*].database`, `.schema`, `.name`, `.relation_name`
- `parent_map` / `child_map` (skip re-deriving from `depends_on` — these are provided pre-flattened)
- Do **not** depend on `compiled_code`, `raw_code`, `columns`, `description`, `docs`, `meta` — these are large, optional for lineage, and the parts most likely to shrink/move (see the size discussion below and the open dbt-core discussion to split descriptions out of manifest.json).

Sources: dbt-core wheels 1.5.0–1.10.0 (`dbt/contracts/graph/manifest.py`, `dbt/artifacts/schemas/manifest/v12/manifest.py`), [Manifest JSON — dbt Developer Hub](https://docs.getdbt.com/reference/artifacts/manifest-json).

### 3. Sources vs models vs tests — table-level edges, and collapsing ephemeral models

- **Sources** are graph *roots* — they never appear as a `key` in `parent_map` (no parents), only as values in other nodes' parent lists, and as keys in `child_map`.
- **Models** appear in `nodes` with `resource_type: "model"`; their `depends_on.nodes` lists immediate parents (sources and/or other models).
- **Tests** (`resource_type: "test"`) are graph *leaves* in a lineage sense — they depend on one or more models/sources but nothing depends on them. For table-level lineage they should be filtered out entirely (they don't produce a table); they're only useful if you also want "which model has data-test coverage" for DQ purposes.
- **`operation`** resource type appears for `on-run-start`/`on-run-end` hooks (our own project has 6 of these from the `grant ...` hooks in `dbt_project.yml`) — also filter out, not lineage-relevant.

**Deriving table-level edges:** walk `parent_map` per model/source node, filtering `nodes` to `resource_type in {model, seed, snapshot}` ∪ all of `sources`, and emit `(parent.relation_name, node.relation_name)`.

**Ephemeral models** — verified in dbt-core source (`dbt/contracts/graph/nodes.py`):
```python
@property
def is_ephemeral(self):
    return self.config.materialized == "ephemeral"
```
An ephemeral model gets **no physical relation** — it's inlined as a CTE into whatever references it, and its `relation_name` is `null`. To collapse it out of a table-level edge list: when building edges, if a parent (or the node itself) `is_ephemeral`, don't emit an edge for it — instead recurse through *its* `depends_on.nodes` (via `parent_map`) until you reach a non-ephemeral model or a source, and connect directly to that. This is exactly the same recursive-substitution dbt itself does when it inlines ephemeral CTEs at compile time.

(Not an issue in our local test project — all 6 models are `view` or `dynamic_table`, none `ephemeral`.)

### 4. Size / streaming concerns

- Our own 6-model / 4-source / 5-test project produces a **611 KB** `manifest.json` (dominated by `compiled_code`/`raw_code` per node, not the graph metadata).
- Real-world large projects: a GitHub issue/discussion thread reports **~3,500 models → ~75 MB** manifests, with the dbt team acknowledging the file "necessarily scales linearly with objects + lines of code," and an open discussion (dbt-core #11080) proposing splitting `description` text out of the manifest because it dominates size in well-documented large projects. Astronomer/Cosmos has filed perf issues where parsing a large manifest per-worker adds 50s+ cold-start latency.
- **Implication for DataQ:** a lineage reader should (a) stream-parse (`ijson` or similar) rather than `json.load()` the whole file when project size is unknown/large, and (b) only pull the minimal-subset fields from §2 — never hold `compiled_code`/`raw_code`/`docs` in memory for the whole graph. dbt's own roadmap direction (Fusion / dbt "2.0") is moving toward Parquet artifacts for exactly this reason — worth a forward-looking note in the design doc, but not needed for the current v12 JSON path.

Sources: [Slim down manifest.json — dbt-core #2169](https://github.com/fishtown-analytics/dbt/issues/2169), [Split descriptions from manifest.json — dbt-core discussion #11080](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/discussions/11080).

### Edges (table-level lineage)

```
DATAQ_DB.RETAIL.customers      -> DATAQ_DB.ANALYTICS_STG.stg_customers
```

This is confirmed both from reading the model SQL (each staging model does `select * from {{ source('retail', X) }}`; each mart does `{{ ref('stg_X') }}` joins) **and** from the real generated `manifest.json`'s `depends_on.nodes` / `parent_map` / `child_map` — they agree exactly (e.g. `child_map["source.dataq_lineage.retail.orders_header"] == ["model.dataq_lineage.stg_orders"]`, `parent_map["model.dataq_lineage.mart_order_revenue"] == ["model.dataq_lineage.stg_order_lines", "model.dataq_lineage.stg_orders"]`).

`mart_order_revenue.order_ts` is explicitly commented as the freshness-monitor anchor column, and `reconciled_subtotal` vs `subtotal` is an intentional intra-order value-reconciliation exposure — both are candidate DataQ check targets referenced in the model SQL comments.

### Artifact upload path

`dbt/upload_artifacts.py` (invoked from `dbt/run.sh` after every `dbt build`, success or failure) uploads `manifest.json` + `run_results.json` to ADLS when `DBT_ARTIFACTS_URI` (format `adls://<account>/<filesystem>/<prefix>`) is set:
- `<prefix>/runs/<UTC-timestamp>/{manifest.json,run_results.json}` — timestamped archive
- `<prefix>/latest/{manifest.json,run_results.json}` — overwritten every run, the stable pointer for a poller

Per CLAUDE.md/memory, the deployed prefix is `raw/dbt`, i.e. artifacts land at ADLS `raw/dbt/latest/manifest.json` — this is the file #596 (lineage ingestion) and #611 (dbt `OrchestrationProvider` poll of `run_results.json`) are designed to consume. `run.sh` uploads artifacts unconditionally regardless of the `dbt build` exit code specifically so a failed run's `run_results.json` is still visible to a failure-observing poller.

### Key file paths (all under `~/Coding/Python/DataQ-harness/dbt/`)
- `dbt_project.yml` — layering config (`+materialized`, `+schema`) and `on-run-end` grants
- `profiles.yml` — Snowflake connection (`DATAQ_LOADER` role, `DATAQ_DB` database)
- `models/staging/_retail__sources.yml` — the 4 source-table defs
- `models/staging/stg_{customers,orders,order_lines,products}.sql` — staging views
- `models/marts/_marts__models.yml` — mart tests (`not_null`/`unique`)
- `models/marts/mart_{order_revenue,customer_orders}.sql` — dynamic-table marts
- `macros/generate_schema_name.sql` — schema-name override macro
- `upload_artifacts.py` / `run.sh` — artifact publish path
- `target/manifest.json` — the actual built artifact used to verify all of the above</result>

# Lineage integration options for DataQ — research report

**Premise:** DataQ pulls lineage from external systems (for blast-radius questions like "which downstream tables are affected by this failing check?") and emits OpenLineage events (so external catalogs can display DataQ's quality results), rather than building its own lineage graph. Constraints: MIT-licensed project (no GPL/AGPL/SSPL/BUSL/Elastic/source-available deps per ADR 0031), vendor-neutral, local-first (docker-compose reference stack), small team.

---

## 1. OpenLineage emission from DataQ (`openlineage-python`)

**License — Apache-2.0, clean.** The OpenLineage repo LICENSE is Apache-2.0 ([github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/LICENSE](https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/LICENSE)) and PyPI declares `Apache-2.0` for `openlineage-python`, latest **1.51.0 released 2026-07-06** — actively maintained ([pypi.org/project/openlineage-python](https://pypi.org/project/openlineage-python/)). The project is an LF AI & Data **Graduate** project (since Sept 2023) ([lfaidata.foundation blog](https://lfaidata.foundation/blog/2023/09/20/lf-ai-data-foundation-announces-graduation-of-openlineage-project/)). Fully compatible with DataQ's MIT policy.

**Transports.** HTTP (retry + compression), Console (the default when unconfigured), File (local or S3/GCS/Azure via the `[fsspec]` extra), Kafka (`[kafka]` extra), plus Composite, Transform, Async-HTTP, and custom transports. Config via constructor, `openlineage.yml`, or env vars — legacy `OPENLINEAGE_URL`/`OPENLINEAGE_API_KEY` or the namespaced `OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__TYPE=http` style; `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` is the global kill switch ([openlineage.io/docs/client/python/configuration](https://openlineage.io/docs/client/python/configuration)). The Console default + `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` means DataQ can ship the emitter dark, off-by-default, with zero infra dependency.

**Event model.** `RunEvent` = one observation of a `Run` (UUID `runId`) of a `Job` (namespace + name) with `inputs`/`outputs` `Dataset` lists; `eventType` ∈ START/RUNNING/COMPLETE/ABORT/FAIL/OTHER — the spec expects exactly one START and one terminal event per run ([openlineage.io/docs/spec/object-model](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/object-model), schema: [spec/OpenLineage.json](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/main/spec/OpenLineage.json)).

**Effort to emit "DQ suite run X read dataset Y" — low, verified code shape** ([openlineage.io/docs/client/python/usage](https://openlineage.io/docs/client/python/usage)):

```python
from openlineage.client import OpenLineageClient
from openlineage.client.event_v2 import Dataset, Job, Run, RunEvent, RunState
from openlineage.client.uuid import generate_new_uuid

client = OpenLineageClient()  # config from env/openlineage.yml
job = Job(namespace="dataq", name="suite.retail_orders")
run = Run(runId=str(generate_new_uuid()))
client.emit(RunEvent(eventType=RunState.START, eventTime=..., run=run, job=job, producer="dataq"))
client.emit(RunEvent(eventType=RunState.COMPLETE, eventTime=..., run=run, job=job, producer="dataq",
                     inputs=[Dataset(namespace="snowflake://acct", name="RETAIL.ORDERS")]))
```

This maps 1:1 onto DataQ's existing run lifecycle (`runs` table start/complete/error) — the emit hooks belong in `run_service`. The only design work is the **dataset naming convention** (OpenLineage namespace/name per datasource type, e.g. `snowflake://<account>` + `db.schema.table`), which the spec standardizes per platform.

**Quality facet — purpose-built for DataQ's use case.** `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` (facet key `dataQualityAssertions`, schema [openlineage.io/spec/facets/1-1-0/DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet.json](https://openlineage.io/spec/facets/1-1-0/DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet.json)) attaches an `assertions[]` array to an input dataset: required `assertion` (e.g. `not_null`, `row_count`) + `success` (bool); optional `column`, `name`, `severity` (`error`|`warn` — maps directly to DataQ's severity tiers), `description`, `expected`, `actual` ([facet docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/facets/dataset-facets/data_quality_assertions)). dbt tests and the Great Expectations integration (`OpenLineageValidationAction` on a GX checkpoint) already populate it ([GX integration docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/great-expectations/)) — so DataQ emitting it from its own GX results is idiomatic, not novel. The older `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet` (rowCount, per-column nullCount/distinctCount/min/max) is a natural carrier for DataQ's `metric_value` monitor-kinds (freshness/volume).

## 2. Marquez (reference consumer)

- **License:** Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/LICENSE)). LF AI & Data **Graduated** project ([lfaidata.foundation/projects/marquez](https://lfaidata.foundation/projects/marquez/)).
- **Lineage pull API — exactly the shape DataQ needs:** `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:<namespace>:<name>&depth=<n>` (depth defaults to 20; nodeId also accepts `job:` and `datasetField:` forms) returns a `graph` of nodes (`type: JOB|DATASET`, `inEdges`, `outEdges`) — a ready-made blast-radius query ([marquezproject.ai/docs/api/get-lineage](https://marquezproject.ai/docs/api/get-lineage/), [openapi.yml](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MarquezProject/marquez/main/spec/openapi.yml)).
- **Ingestion:** native OpenLineage receiver — `POST /api/v1/lineage` accepts RunEvents directly; Marquez is the OpenLineage reference implementation ([record-lineage docs](https://marquezproject.ai/docs/api/record-lineage/)).
- **Deployment weight — light by catalog standards:** two containers (Java/Dropwizard API + `postgres:14`), optional React web UI third; official [docker-compose.yml](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/blob/main/docker-compose.yml) + Helm chart in-repo. No embedded/in-memory mode exists (absence-of-evidence — no doc states it, none found), so Postgres is mandatory — but DataQ's compose stack already runs Postgres.
- **Maintenance caveat:** repo is active (commits into 2026-04, push 2026-07-06) but the **last tagged release is 0.50.0 from 2024-10-24** ([releases](https://github.com/MarquezProject/marquez/releases)) — a stalled release cadence worth noting, though as a dev-time reference consumer (not a runtime dependency) the risk is low.

## 3. DataHub

- **License:** core Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/datahub-project/datahub/blob/master/LICENSE)); Python SDK `acryl-datahub` also Apache-2.0 ([pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub](https://pypi.org/project/acryl-datahub/)). **No licensing controversy found** despite targeted search — the one soft flag is the 2025 Acryl Data → "DataHub" corporate rebrand + "DataHub Cloud" paid product, creating OSS/vendor/product name overlap ([Series B announcement](https://datahub.com/news/series-b-announcement/), [TechCrunch on Acryl](https://techcrunch.com/2021/06/23/acryl-data-commercializing-linkedins-metadata-tool-datahub-emerges-from-stealth-with-9m-from-8vc-linkedin-and-insight/)).
- **Lineage read:** GraphQL `dataset(urn:...)` + `lineage(input: {direction: UPSTREAM|DOWNSTREAM})`, multi-hop via `scrollAcrossLineage`; OpenAPI v3 supports lineage PATCH edits ([lineage tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/lineage), [OpenAPI guide](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/openapi/openapi-usage-guide)).
- **Assertions push — the strongest external-DQ model of the three catalogs:** Assertion entities with `source.type = EXTERNAL` + timeseries `AssertionRunEvent` (pass/fail + severity per run), pushed via `DatahubRestEmitter` or the `upsertCustomAssertion` GraphQL mutation ([custom-assertions tutorial](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/api/tutorials/custom-assertions), [assertion metamodel](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/generated/metamodel/entities/assertion)).
- **OpenLineage ingestion — native HTTP receiver:** `POST <GMS>/openapi/openlineage/api/v1/lineage` accepts OpenLineage events directly ([docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/lineage/openlineage)) — DataQ's emitter reaches DataHub with zero extra code.
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** quickstart deploys Kafka + Zookeeper + MySQL + OpenSearch + GMS + frontend + Actions; documented minimum 2 CPU / 8 GB RAM / 13 GB disk ([quickstart](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/quickstart)). Too heavy for DataQ's local-first compose reference stack.
- **Maturity:** very active — v1.6.0 shipped 2026-05-21 ([releases](https://docs.datahub.com/docs/releases)).

## 4. OpenMetadata

- **License — the disqualifier.** The server core is Apache-2.0 ([LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/LICENSE)), **but the Python SDK/ingestion framework `openmetadata-ingestion` — the surface DataQ would actually integrate through — is under the "Collate Community License Agreement, Version 1.0"** ([pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion](https://pypi.org/project/openmetadata-ingestion/), [ingestion/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/ingestion/LICENSE)). I independently verified the license text: it contains an "Excluded Purpose" non-compete — *"'Excluded Purpose' means making available any software-as-a-service, platform-as-a-service, infrastructure-as-a-service or other similar online service that competes with Collate products or services."* That is exactly the BUSL/Elastic-style source-available category DataQ's policy prohibits (CLAUDE.md §11, ADR 0031). The UI carries the same license ([openmetadata-ui/LICENSE](https://github.com/open-metadata/OpenMetadata/blob/main/openmetadata-ui/LICENSE)). Talking to the Apache-2.0 REST API directly (no SDK dependency) would technically stay clean, but the license posture is a standing risk.
- **APIs (for completeness):** lineage `GET /api/v1/lineage/<entityType>/name/<fqn>?upstreamDepth=&downstreamDepth=` ([API reference](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/api-reference/lineage)); DQ push `PUT /api/v1/dataQuality/testCases/{fqn}/testCaseResult` ([custom-tests guide](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/how-to-guides/data-quality-observability/quality/custom-tests)) — genuinely good DQ-native APIs.
- **OpenLineage support:** exists but **beta and Kafka/Kinesis-only** — no plain HTTP receiver; requires entities pre-registered ([OpenLineage connector docs](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/connectors/pipeline/openlineage)).
- **Deployment weight — heavy:** MySQL/Postgres + Elasticsearch + bundled Airflow; stated minimum 6 GiB RAM / 4 vCPU ([local Docker deployment](https://docs.open-metadata.org/v1.12.x/quick-start/local-docker-deployment)). Active project (1.13.1, 2026-06-27; Collate-backed).

## 5. Microsoft Purview (low priority — Azure wind-down)

- **Lineage read:** Apache Atlas-compatible API — `GET {endpoint}/datamap/api/atlas/v2/lineage/{guid}?direction=BOTH`; Microsoft's FAQ states Atlas compatibility explicitly ([lineage REST API](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-gov-api-create-lineage-relationships), [FAQ](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/data-governance-faq)).
- **OpenLineage status:** no general-purpose native receiver. Airflow lineage is public **preview** via a bring-your-own Event Hub + `openlineage-airflow` Kafka transport, limited Airflow versions/sources, no column lineage ([Airflow lineage preview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/data-map-lineage-airflow)); the Databricks path is a 2022 solution accelerator ([Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator](https://github.com/microsoft/Purview-ADB-Lineage-Solution-Accelerator)). Roadmap language only, not GA.
- **Azure-only:** Purview is an Azure/Fabric-hosted service requiring an Azure subscription ([account creation docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/purview/create-microsoft-purview-portal)). Given DataQ's Azure wind-down (~2026-07-25), this belongs behind a future adapter seam, not in v-next scope.

## 6. Orchestrators as direct pull sources

- **Airflow:** `apache-airflow-providers-openlineage` is the official ASF provider (in-repo since Airflow 2.7, superseding community `openlineage-airflow`), Apache-2.0, auto-emits per-task RunEvents via listener hooks; latest **2.18.1 (2026-06-22)**, 2.19.0rc1 already cut — healthy cadence ([provider docs](https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/stable/index.html), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/apache-airflow-providers-openlineage/)). Key implication: DataQ doesn't "pull from Airflow" — the user configures the provider to **push into whatever OpenLineage receiver DataQ points it at**. This mirrors DataQ's existing Airflow-callback pattern (user-deployed snippet, `integrations/airflow/`).
- **dbt:** still wrapper-based — `dbt-ol run` (package `openlineage-dbt`, Apache-2.0, **1.51.0, 2026-07-06** — versioned with the monorepo, actively maintained) parses `manifest.json` + `run_results.json` post-run and emits events ([integration docs](https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/), [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/openlineage-dbt/)). **No first-party dbt Labs support has shipped**; the feature request [dbt-core#11750](https://github.com/dbt-labs/dbt-core/issues/11750) (opened 2025-06-18) is open with no maintainer commitment. Notably, DataQ's existing dbt provider (ADR 0029) already polls those same artifacts — the manifest's node graph is a lineage source DataQ already has plumbing for.

---

## Recommended first slice (small team, local-first, MIT-safe)

### (b) Quality-facet publishing — build this FIRST

1. **Emit OpenLineage RunEvents with `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` from `run_service`** using `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0, one pip dep, zero infra). START on run dispatch; COMPLETE/FAIL with the suite's target as an input `Dataset` carrying the assertions facet (check name → `assertion`, pass/fail → `success`, DataQ severity → `severity`, observed/expected → `actual`/`expected`; `metric_value` monitors → `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet`). Config via standard `OPENLINEAGE_URL`/`OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__*` env vars, console default, `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored — off-by-default, no new required service. This one emitter reaches Marquez, DataHub (native HTTP OL receiver), and Kafka consumers **without any per-catalog code**, which is the whole vendor-neutrality argument.
2. **Add Marquez to the reference docker-compose as the demo consumer** (2 containers; reuses the Postgres pattern already in the stack). Apache-2.0, purpose-built OL receiver, and it doubles as the verification harness for slice 1.

*Why first:* lowest effort (days, not weeks — the hooks map 1:1 to the existing run lifecycle), zero license risk, zero mandatory infra, and it makes DataQ a good citizen of the ecosystem before asking anything of it.

### (a) Blast-radius lineage pull — build SECOND, behind a seam

3. **A `LineageProvider` abstraction (mirroring `OrchestrationProvider`) with Marquez as the reference impl:** `get_lineage(dataset) → graph` backed by `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:<ns>:<name>&depth=N`. Local-first story: Airflow's OL provider and `dbt-ol` push into Marquez; DataQ pulls the graph back out to answer "check failed on X → what's downstream?". Note Marquez's stalled release tags (0.50.0, Oct 2024) in the ADR as an accepted risk.
4. **A dbt-manifest lineage impl as the zero-infra fallback:** DataQ's ADR-0029 dbt provider already polls `manifest.json`/`run_results.json`; parsing the manifest's DAG gives model-level blast radius with **no lineage backend at all** — the true local-first floor.
5. **DataHub impl of the same seam — deferred:** best-in-class external-assertion push API and a native OL HTTP receiver (so slice 1 already works against it), Apache-2.0 SDK; but the Kafka+OpenSearch+MySQL footprint (8 GB RAM minimum) disqualifies it from the compose reference stack. Add the GraphQL lineage-pull impl only when a user brings an existing DataHub.
6. **OpenMetadata — do not integrate via SDK:** the `openmetadata-ingestion` package's Collate Community License (source-available, non-compete) violates DataQ's ADR-0031 policy. If ever needed, use the Apache-2.0 server REST API directly and record the decision in an ADR.
7. **Purview — park it:** Atlas-API read is feasible later behind the same `LineageProvider` seam, but preview-grade OL support + Azure-only hosting + DataQ's Azure wind-down make it last.

**Bottom line:** the emitter-plus-facet slice (openlineage-python → any receiver) is the highest-leverage, license-cleanest first move; Marquez is the right local-first reference consumer despite its slow release tagging; DataHub is the "bring your own catalog" target that works with the same emitter; OpenMetadata is blocked at the SDK layer by a source-available license; Purview is a deferred Azure-only adapter.</result>
