<result># OpenLineage Dataset Naming Specification — Research Report

**Primary source note:** the canonical naming spec lives at `website/docs/spec/naming.md` in the OpenLineage repo, rendered at **https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming**. The old `spec/Naming.md` file you pointed at is explicitly marked obsolete — I fetched it directly and its entire content is now just a pointer:

> "This document is obsolete, please look at the [OpenLineage Naming Conventions](https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming)"
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/Naming.md

All namespace/name templates below are taken verbatim from the current spec table, then cross-checked against the actual reference implementations (`client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py` and `client/java/.../dataset/Naming.java`), which are attr/Builder classes whose `get_namespace()`/`get_name()` implementations are byte-identical to the spec table — i.e., these two files *are* the spec, expressed as code.

---

## 1–2. Per-datasource namespace + name construction, with case/quoting/defaults notes

| Datasource | Namespace template | Name template | Concrete example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Snowflake** | `snowflake://{organization_name}-{account_name}` (preferred, orgname-account format) **or** `snowflake://{account_locator}(.{region}).{cloud}` (legacy locator format) | `{database}.{schema}.{table}` | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` — legacy form: `snowflake://xy12345.us-east-2.aws` / `MYDB.PUBLIC.ORDERS` |
| **Databricks Unity Catalog** | `unitycatalog://{host}` or `unitycatalog://{host}:{port}` | `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}` | `unitycatalog://adb-123456789.0.azuredatabricks.net` / `main.sales.orders` |
| **ADLS Gen2 (abfss://)** | `abfss://{container_name}@{service_name}.dfs.core.windows.net` | `{path}` | `abfss://raw@mydatalake.dfs.core.windows.net` / `retail/orders/2026/07/10/` |
| **AWS S3** | `s3://{bucket_name}` | `{object_key}` | `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/part-00000.parquet` |
| **Apache Iceberg — generic REST catalog** | the catalog's raw REST URI, taken verbatim from `catalog.uri` config (no `iceberg://` prefix; whatever scheme+host the REST endpoint has) | `{iceberg_namespace}.{table}` (Iceberg's own multi-level namespace, dot-joined, exactly as Spark's `Identifier.toString()` renders it) | `https://iceberg-rest.example.com:8181` / `retail.purchase_orders` |
| **Apache Iceberg — Snowflake-hosted Iceberg REST catalog** (special-cased) | `snowflake://{account_identifier}` — reuses the Snowflake namespace rule above, not a generic REST namespace | `{warehouse}.{table}` where `warehouse` = the Iceberg `CatalogProperties.WAREHOUSE_LOCATION` conf, and the whole name is **upper-cased** | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `RETAIL_WH.PURCHASE_ORDERS` |
| **dbt on Snowflake (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol)** | `snowflake://{fix_account_name(profiles.yml "account")}` — identical normalization function to the spec's account-identifier rule | `{database}.{schema}.{table_or_alias}` (dot-join of non-empty parts, using dbt manifest's own casing) | `snowflake://myorg-myaccount` / `ANALYTICS.MARTS.FCT_ORDERS` |

Sources: spec table — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming) · Python reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py · Java reference impl — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/dataset/Naming.java · Snowflake account normalization — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · Unity Catalog Spark handler + its unit test (asserts scheme dropped, path dropped, host:port kept, http/https collapse to the same namespace) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java and https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/test/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandlerNamingTest.java · Iceberg catalog-type handlers — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/RestCatalogTypeHandler.java , .../BaseCatalogTypeHandler.java , and .../SnowflakeCatalogTypeHandler.java · dbt namespace/name derivation — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py (methods `extract_namespace` and `_format_dataset_name`) · dbt integration overview — https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/

**For reference, the other datasource types in the same spec table** (useful context, not asked for directly but confirms the pattern family): BigQuery `bigquery` / `{project}.{dataset}.{table}`; Redshift `redshift://{cluster}.{region}:{port}` / `{database}.{schema}.{table}`; Postgres/MSSQL/Oracle/DB2/CrateDB same 3-part shape; MySQL/Hive/Teradata/OceanBase drop schema entirely (`{database}.{table}` — these engines have no schema level); Trino `trino://{host}:{port}` / `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}`; GCS `gs://{bucket}` / `{object_key}`; HDFS `hdfs://{namenode_host}:{namenode_port}` / `{path}`; local filesystem `file` / `{path}`; remote filesystem `file://{host}` / `{path}`; Kafka `kafka://{bootstrap_host}:{port}` / `{topic}`; PubSub `pubsub` / `topic:{projectId}:{topicId}`.

### Case sensitivity, quoting, and default-schema omission

- **No spec-level case-folding rule exists.** The name components are simply whatever the source system reports (e.g., dbt's `manifest.json` `database`/`schema`/`alias` fields, taken as-is). Practically, this means Snowflake-backed datasets will render **upper-case** unless the underlying objects were created with quoted mixed/lower-case identifiers, because Snowflake itself upper-cases unquoted identifiers — the OpenLineage layer does not re-case anything.
- **Quoting is stripped, not preserved.** `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes()` explicitly removes surrounding double-quotes from identifiers before they go into a dataset name/namespace, with the documented rationale: *"double quotes are used as delimiters to preserve case sensitivity or allow special characters in identifiers... quotes are not part of the actual identifier name and should be removed for normalized dataset and column names."* (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java)
- **Schema/database omission is engine-driven, not a generic spec default.** The spec doesn't have a universal "default schema" rule — instead, each per-engine name template is fixed at either 2 or 3 segments matching that engine's actual object hierarchy (MySQL/Hive/Teradata have no schema concept, so their template is literally 2-segment). Where a value legitimately can be missing (Snowflake account locator's region/cloud), the account-normalization function inserts an explicit default rather than omitting the segment: bare locator `xy12345` → `xy12345.us-west-1.aws` (defaults to AWS US-West Oregon); `xy12345.us-east-1` → `xy12345.us-east-1.aws` (defaults cloud to `aws`). Source: docstrings in `SnowflakeUtils.parseAccountIdentifier` / `fix_account_name` (both links above).

---

## 3. Gotchas — cross-environment ambiguity

**Yes — different Snowflake accounts (DEV vs QA) are, by design, distinct namespaces, hence distinct dataset identities.** This isn't just an inference; it's explicit in the spec text itself:

> "Snowflake is a special case where we don't have a single standardized namespace, due to its account identifier model... If you switch formats later, existing lineage nodes won't connect to new ones."
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md

The maintainer's design rationale (from a live GitHub discussion questioning exactly this kind of ambiguity) draws the line explicitly on **physical/tenant isolation**:

> BigQuery gets a single global namespace (`bigquery`, no project ID) because *"you can query other projects from your project: `SELECT ... FROM project.dataset.table`"* — projects are tenants inside one addressable system. AWS Glue and Spanner *do* fold the account/project ID into the namespace because they're *"physically separate systems"* with regional/instance-level isolation.
> — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/discussions/4251 (maintainer `mobuchowski`)

A DEV Snowflake account and a QA Snowflake account are exactly this second case — physically separate authentication/compute realms reachable at different hostnames — so under the spec's own logic they are *correctly* distinct namespaces (`snowflake://dev-org-account` vs `snowflake://qa-org-account`), not a modeling bug.

**What practitioners do to group "the same logical asset" across environments — the spec deliberately does not solve this at the identifier level:**
- **`SymlinksDatasetFacet`** — the one first-party mechanism for "this is also known as X" — but its documented use case is engine-level aliasing (Hive table addressed by file path vs metastore name; the same dataset seen via FQDN vs short hostname), *not* cross-environment/cross-tenant grouping. Schema: `identifiers: [{namespace, name, type}]`. Source: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/SymlinksDatasetFacet.json (rendered: https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/facets/dataset-facets/symlinks/)
- **Custom/consumer-side facets or tags** — the discussion thread's maintainer endorses solving analogous ambiguity (MySQL across multiple hosts/datacenters) via either (a) folding a discriminator into the host-naming strategy itself, or (b) a facet-based extension, calling both "valid" — i.e., there's no canonical spec answer, it's pushed to the implementer.
- **Downstream lineage/catalog tools do the grouping**, not OpenLineage identifiers themselves — e.g., matching same `{schema}.{table}` name across differing namespaces under one business-glossary/logical-asset entity, or tagging datasets with an `environment` custom facet/tag that a UI groups on. This is consistent with `TagsDatasetFacet` existing as a generic escape hatch (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/TagsDatasetFacet.json) but there is no spec-blessed "environment" or "logical-asset-id" facet as of this research.

**Design-doc implication for DataQ:** if DataQ wants "flag the same suite/check across DEV/QA/UAT Snowflake" to read as one logical asset in any OpenLineage-facing surface, that grouping needs to live in DataQ's own domain model (e.g., a `logical_asset` tag/facet DataQ attaches), not in the OpenLineage namespace/name — the namespace will correctly and unavoidably differ per account.

---

## 4. File-pattern datasets (directories, daily-partitioned CSVs, globs)

The spec itself only goes as far as: for every blob/file-store backend (S3, GCS, ABFSS, HDFS, local, remote filesystem, WASBS), **name = `{path}`**, full stop — there is no spec-level notion of a glob or partition wildcard in the name template. Confirmed by re-checking every storage row in the canonical table (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md) and by grepping the full facets registry (`spec/facets/*.json`, https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/tree/main/spec/facets) — there is **no `PartitionDatasetFacet`** in the first-party facet set.

The convention that fills this gap comes from how the reference Spark integration actually resolves a dataset identifier for a partitioned/pattern read: it uses the relation's/table's base **`location`** (the directory root Spark/Hive resolved the partitioned table or file-source read against), not per-partition-file paths — I confirmed this by reading `PathUtils.java` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/shared/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark/agent/util/PathUtils.java), which contains no partition- or glob-splitting logic at all; the base directory is what gets passed in as `path` for the `S3`/`HDFS`/`GCS`/`ABFSS` naming classes shown above. So the practitioner/implementation convention is exactly what you hypothesized:

- **Dataset = the directory/prefix** (e.g. `s3://dataq-lake` / `retail/purchase_orders/` — not `retail/purchase_orders/dt=2026-07-10/part-00000.csv`).
- **Partition structure is not modeled as separate datasets.** The two facets available to describe *how* that directory is physically laid out are:
  - `StorageDatasetFacet` — `storageLayer` (`iceberg`/`delta`) + `fileFormat` (`parquet`/`orc`/`avro`/`json`/`csv`/`text`/`xml`) — https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/StorageDatasetFacet.json
  - `SchemaDatasetFacet` — partition *columns* (e.g. a Hive-style `dt` column) show up here as ordinary schema fields, not as a separate partition concept.
- There is no first-party facet for "list of partition values covered by this run" — teams that need that level of granularity either encode it in a custom facet or in `OutputStatisticsOutputDatasetFacet`/row-count-style facets, outside the core naming spec.

---

## 5. dbt ↔ OpenLineage (openlineage-dbt / dbt-ol) — exact naming behavior for Snowflake, verified against source

I read the actual method bodies in `integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py` (the shared processor both `dbt-ol` and the Airflow dbt-Cloud/Core operators build on) rather than relying on docs, since the docs page (https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/) doesn't spell this out.

**Namespace** — `DbtArtifactProcessor.extract_namespace(profile)`:
```python
if self.adapter_type == Adapter.SNOWFLAKE:
    return f"snowflake://{fix_account_name(profile['account'])}"
```
`profile['account']` is read straight out of the user's `profiles.yml` target block (dbt's own `account:` key), and `fix_account_name` (https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py) is the **identical** normalization routine used by the core Snowflake naming spec: if the account string contains a hyphen it's treated as already being `orgname-accountname` and passed through unchanged; otherwise it's treated as a legacy locator and gets `.us-west-1.aws` appended (bare locator) or `.aws` appended (locator+region only). This is exactly the same defaulting behavior documented on the canonical Snowflake namespace row.

**Name** — `DbtArtifactProcessor._format_dataset_name(database, schema, table)`:
```python
def _format_dataset_name(self, database, schema, table):
    return ".".join(list(filter(None, [database, schema, table])))
```
called with `node.metadata_node["database"]`, `["schema"]`, and (for models) `["alias"]` or (for sources) `["name"]` — i.e. taken verbatim, in whatever case, from dbt's own `manifest.json`. No re-casing, no quote-stripping happens on the dbt side (unlike the Java `SnowflakeUtils.stripQuotes` used elsewhere in the client).

**Practical implication for matching byte-for-byte:** for DataQ's Snowflake connector to emit dataset identifiers that line up with what `dbt-ol` would emit for the *same* physical tables, it must (a) run the identical account-string normalization (hyphenated org-account passthrough vs. legacy-locator defaulting to `us-west-1`/`aws`), and (b) use the database/schema/table names exactly as Snowflake's catalog returns them unquoted (which will be upper-case for the overwhelming majority of real warehouses) rather than lower-casing or otherwise normalizing them.

Sources: https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py · https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py · https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/ (env-var config surface, e.g. `OPENLINEAGE_NAMESPACE` for job namespace, `OPENLINEAGE_DBT_USE_EXTENDED_JOB_NAME` for job *name* — neither affects dataset namespace/name, which is always profile/manifest-derived per the above).

---

### Full list of primary sources cited above
- https://openlineage.io/docs/spec/naming
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/Naming.md (obsolete redirect)
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/python/src/openlineage/client/naming/dataset.py
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/dataset/Naming.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/client/java/src/main/java/io/openlineage/client/utils/SnowflakeUtils.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/snowflake.py
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/processor.py
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/common/src/openlineage/common/provider/dbt/local.py
- https://openlineage.io/docs/integrations/dbt/
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/test/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandlerNamingTest.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/RestCatalogTypeHandler.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/BaseCatalogTypeHandler.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/spark3/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark3/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/iceberg/SnowflakeCatalogTypeHandler.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/integration/spark/shared/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark/agent/util/PathUtils.java
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/SymlinksDatasetFacet.json
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/blob/main/spec/facets/StorageDatasetFacet.json
- https://github.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/discussions/4251
- https://openlineage.io/blog/whats-in-a-namespace/</result>

result.output: Todos have been modified successfully. Ensure that you continue to use the todo list to track your progress. Please proceed with the current tasks if applicable

1	# Post-v1 notes — Expectation expansion, marketplace & LLM-assisted authoring (deferred design)
2	
3	> **Status: deferred to post-v1.** Captured so the design intent isn't lost. None of
4	> this is a v1 blocker — the v1 DQ loop (checks → results → trends → freshness/volume
5	> monitors → alerts → MCP) stands on its own. These are the "intelligence" layer on
6	> top of it.
7	>
8	> **The big enabler (already true in v1):** there is **no server-side expectation
9	> allowlist** — [`gx_runner`](datasources/gx_runner.py) title-cases *any*
10	> `expectation_type` string into a GX class, and `checks.config` is free-form JSONB.
11	> So "add an expectation" / "wire in a check" is mostly a **frontend-catalog + config-
12	> validation** problem, not a backend-engine one. That's why the items below are
13	> cheaper than they look.
14	
15	## The four themes
16	
17	### 1. Implement the 5 high-ROI GX built-ins
18	**Feasibility: High · Effort: S–M · No new infra.**
19	
20	- **4 of 5 are catalog-only** entries in
21	  [`frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts`](../frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts):
22	  - **`mostly`** — tolerance on any existing expectation ("95% not-null"). One optional
23	    field; GX still emits `unexpected_percent`, so it bands under ADR 0016 unchanged.
24	    **Highest ROI / lowest cost** — do first.
25	  - **Compound / cross-column** — `expect_compound_columns_to_be_unique` (multi-column
26	    primary keys), `expect_column_pair_values_a_to_be_greater_than_b` (e.g.
27	    `end_date > start_date`), `expect_multicolumn_sum_to_equal`.
28	  - **Type** — `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` / `in_type_list`.
29	  - **Set relations / date format** — `expect_column_distinct_values_to_be_in_set` /
30	    `to_contain_set`, `expect_column_values_to_match_strftime_format`.
31	- **The 5th — aggregate stats** (`expect_column_{mean,median,sum,stdev,min,max}_to_be_between`)
32	  is the **design decision, not a free add.** They don't emit `unexpected_percent`; the
33	  result is a **scalar in `observed_value`** and they're **two-sided** (too low *or* too
34	  high fails). That's the *volume-monitor* shape, not the GX unexpected-% shape — so
35	  aggregates likely belong on the **monitor-kind `metric_value` path** (ADR 0012, the
36	  freshness/volume engine, #426) as a 3rd monitor kind, **not** as a GX-banded
37	  expectation. Decide deliberately before building.
38	
39	### 2. LLM SQL generator for custom-SQL checks (`UnexpectedRowsExpectation`)
40	**Feasibility: High · Effort: M · Best-aligned of the four.**
41	
42	- NL rule → LLM-generated SQL → existing **custom-SQL guardrails** (ADR 0019:
43	  read-only single-statement validation + SQL-datasource gating + least-privilege role)
44	  → **dry-run preview** before save.
45	- **Principle: trust LLM SQL no more than user SQL** — run it through the *same*
46	  validator + dry-run. That neutralizes "LLM writes `DROP TABLE`" for free.
47	- Real risk is *correctness* (hallucinated columns), mitigated by feeding **schema +
48	  column-profiler stats** as context (we already have the profiler).
49	
50	### 3. LLM curated check suggestions for a suite
51	**Feasibility: High · Effort: M.**
52	
53	- "GX Data Assistant, but LLM-driven and constrained to *our* catalog vocabulary." The
54	  input it needs already exists — the **column profiler** (null %, distinct counts,
55	  distributions).
56	- Must use **structured / schema-constrained output**: the LLM emits checks in the
57	  catalog's exact `expectation_type` + config schema (derive a tool schema from the
58	  catalog), so it can't suggest something the runner can't run. Pairs with #1 (richer
59	  catalog ⇒ better suggestions) and the MCP work.
60	
61	### 4. Plug-and-play expectation marketplace — **scope = 4a (curated superset)**
62	**Feasibility: High.**
63	
64	- **Chosen (4a): a vetted, server-served superset of GX's ~50 built-ins** — the existing
65	  catalog pattern scaled up. The frontend picks; the backend already runs it.
66	- **Not doing (4b): an open marketplace** of community/contrib or arbitrary custom
67	  `Expectation` classes. "Backend wires it in" from user input = a **code-execution /
68	  supply-chain risk**; such classes would have to be installed in the image + pinned +
69	  reviewed, not loaded dynamically. Against the anti-supply-chain posture.
70	- **Useful irony:** doing 4a *safely* means **adding the server-side allowlist** v1
71	  lacks — flip the "no allowlist" convenience into a vetted catalog the backend validates
72	  submitted `expectation_type`s against. (This also hardens #2/#3's generated output.)
73	
74	## Sequencing
75	
76	```
77	#1 built-ins ──► richer vocabulary ──► #3 suggestions
78	   (cheap, now-ish)                        ▲
79	                                           │ both need:
80	#4a curated marketplace (extends #1)       │
81	                              LLM-client seam (NEW) ──► #2 SQL-gen
82	```
83	
84	Suggested order: **#1** (immediate value, no infra, unblocks #3's vocabulary) → **#4a**
85	(extends #1; adds the validation allowlist) → **LLM seam + #2** (highest user "wow,"
86	guardrails already done) → **#3** (needs the seam *and* the richer catalog).
87	
88	## LLM integration model (proposed — not locked)
89	
90	**#2 and #3 share one capability v1 doesn't have: DataQ calling an LLM *outbound*.**
91	(Note the W7 **MCP server is the opposite direction** — it exposes *our* tools to
92	external LLM clients; it does not let us call one.) The recommended shape mirrors the
93	seams the app already has (`ConnectionAdapter`, `SecretStore`, `OrchestrationProvider`):
94	
95	**An `LLMProvider` seam — admin-configured, default-OFF, customer brings their own
96	credential/endpoint.**
97	
98	- **Configuration scope = workspace-admin, not per-user.** Single-tenant + customer-
99	  deployed (BYOL, ADR 0013): the admin (`WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS`) configures **one**
100	  provider (provider + endpoint + model + credential), credential stored in the
101	  **`SecretStore`** (Key Vault is one impl). Per-user keys are friction for a team tool —
102	  defer as an optional power-user *override*, not the primary model.
103	- **Default off + graceful degradation.** The app must fully work with no LLM configured
104	  (hand-authored checks, the catalog, custom SQL). LLM features are additive.
105	- **Pluggable concrete impls behind the seam (anti-lock-in, ADR 0010/0013 — no hardcoded
106	  vendor in business logic):**
107	  1. **Anthropic API** — default impl (CLAUDE.md: default to the latest Claude models).
108	  2. **Azure AI Foundry / Azure OpenAI** — for customers already on Azure.
109	  3. **AWS Bedrock** — for customers on AWS.
110	  4. **Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint** — this is the **"local LLM" answer**: we do
111	     **not** bake a model server or HF download into the app image (against the slim-image
112	     ADR 0025 — GPU/ops/size). Instead the customer runs their own inference server
113	     (**Ollama / vLLM / HF TGI**, which is where they'd pull an HF model) and points
114	     DataQ at its OpenAI-compatible URL. "Local LLM" = an endpoint impl, uniform with the
115	     others.
116	  - Most of these speak OpenAI-compatible or a thin adapter, so the seam is largely
117	    `{base_url, api_key, model, auth_adapter}` + a capability flag for tool-use/structured
118	    output (some local models do JSON-schema output poorly → prompt-based JSON fallback).
119	- **Cost ownership = the customer's.** BYOL means the customer pays their own LLM bill via
120	  their own key/endpoint. **Never proxy through an Anthropic key we own** — that turns it
121	  into hosted SaaS + our cost (contra ADR 0013).
122	- **Data residency is the deciding axis** for *which* provider a customer picks, and it's
123	  a hard requirement: apply the **same PII discipline** as the rest of the app — send the
124	  LLM **schema + aggregate profiler stats only, never raw sample rows / PII-column
125	  values** (reuse the redaction seam). The local-endpoint impl exists precisely for
126	  customers who can't send even schema externally.
127	- **Where it runs:** the **Celery worker** (async, already holds secret access) — not the
128	  request path. An ADR should record the seam + default impl + the "BYO credential,
129	  default-off, no-proxy" posture.
130	
131	## Guiding principle
132	
133	Build the intelligence layer **on top of** a solid DQ loop, constrained to the catalog
134	vocabulary and the existing safety seams (custom-SQL read-only validation, PII redaction,
135	SecretStore, anti-lock-in). The LLM is an **authoring assistant**, never an unchecked
136	execution path — generated SQL and suggested checks go through the **same** validation a
137	human's would.
138

content.text: Now drafting the design doc — I have the voice. Writing `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md`:

input.content:
# Post-v1 notes — Assets, lineage & incidents (gap G-d design)

> **Status: design for the next cycle's build (the #596 deliverable — no code in this
> PR).** Gap G-d is the "usable in anger" gap: runs and alerts exist, but "what broke
> downstream / who owns it / when was it resolved" doesn't — and that triad is what DQ
> products are bought for. This doc pins the three objects (asset, lineage edge,
> incident), their identity model, and the build order; the phase-1 issues it files are
> the next cycle's generator input. Decision record: ADR
> [0034](adr/0034-asset-entity-openlineage-identity-lineage-pull.md).
>
> Related: **#596** (this doc), Themes 2/3/5/14 in
> [context/post-v1-roadmap.md](../context/post-v1-roadmap.md), ADR 0012 (monitor
> kinds), ADR 0027/0033 (authz axes), ADR 0029 (dbt provider), ADR 0031 (licensing).

## 1. The asset entity — the one migration everything rides

Today "the table" exists only implicitly inside `Suite.target` (one JSONB blob,
resolved per connection type in `run_target.py`). Lineage needs asset *nodes*, the
asset page needs asset *identity*, incidents need something to *attach to*, and
catalog sync needs an entity to *map to* — one `assets` table serves all four, so it
ships first (phase 1) and alone.

- **Identity = the OpenLineage dataset naming spec** (`namespace` + `name`,
  unique together), adopted verbatim so Theme-14 emission/pull interop is a no-op
  rather than a mapping layer. Canonical resolution from `resolve_target()` output:

  | Connection type | Namespace | Name |
  |---|---|---|
  | `snowflake` | `snowflake://{org}-{account}` (normalized account identifier) | `DB.SCHEMA.TABLE` |
  | `unity_catalog` | `unitycatalog://{workspace_host}` | `catalog.schema.table` |
  | `adls_gen2` | `abfss://{container}@{account}.dfs.core.windows.net` | `{path or pattern base dir}` |
  | `s3` | `s3://{bucket}` | `{key or pattern base dir}` |
  | `iceberg` | catalog URI verbatim (REST) / `file` (local warehouse) | `{namespace}.{table}` |

- **Normalization rules (byte-compatibility with `openlineage-dbt` / Spark):** strip
  identifier quotes, keep the case the engine's catalog returns (Snowflake ⇒ upper),
  run the same Snowflake account normalization the OL clients use (hyphenated
  org-account passes through; legacy locator gets region/cloud defaults appended).
  A flat-file **pattern** asset is the pattern's literal base directory/prefix — the
  Spark-integration convention (dataset = directory, partitions are not datasets).
- **DEV vs QA is two assets, by design.** The spec keys namespace on physical/tenant
  isolation, so the same logical table via two accounts *correctly* differs. Grouping
  across environments is DataQ's job, not the identifier's: the asset row carries
  `env` (from the connection), and the UI groups by `name + env` when asked. No
  internal ID scheme, no cross-env merging at the identity level.
- **Shape (indicative):** `assets(id, namespace, name, env, connection_id FK
  nullable — provenance hint, not identity, first_seen, last_seen,
  owner_user_id nullable — §4)`; unique `(namespace, name)`. Suites resolve their
  target to an `asset_id` on save; runs stamp the resolved `asset_id` at dispatch
  (targets can change; run history shouldn't rewrite). Backfill migration resolves
  every existing `Suite.target`.

## 2. Lineage — pull, don't build (three slices, in order)

We do not build a lineage graph engine (Theme 14 decision, reaffirmed). Three slices,
each independently shippable:

1. **Emit OpenLineage first** — `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0) hooks in
   `run_service`: START on dispatch, COMPLETE/FAIL on finish, the suite's asset as an
   input `Dataset` carrying **`DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet`** (check →
   `assertion`, outcome → `success`, our severity tiers → `severity`) and the
   metrics facet for `metric_value` monitor kinds. Console transport by default,
   `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored — **dark by default, zero new required infra**, and
   one emitter reaches Marquez, DataHub, and Kafka consumers with no per-catalog code.
2. **dbt-manifest edges — the zero-infra blast-radius floor.** The ADR-0029 provider
   already polls `run_results.json`; the same 3-scheme artifact reader fetches the
   sibling `manifest.json` (present at `raw/dbt/latest/` today, unconsumed). Parse the
   **minimal stable subset** (`parent_map`/`child_map`, per-node
   `database`/`schema`/`alias`/`relation_name`/`resource_type`/`config.materialized`;
   never `compiled_code`/`raw_code` — stream-parse, manifests hit tens of MB at
   thousands of models), gate on `metadata.dbt_schema_version` (v12 is stable across
   dbt-core 1.8→1.11), collapse ephemeral models (null `relation_name`) by recursing
   to their nearest physical ancestors, drop tests/operations. Edges land in a small
   **`lineage_edges` cache** (`upstream_asset_id`, `downstream_asset_id`, `source`,
   `last_seen`) — a cache of external truth refreshed per poll, not a graph we author.
   Harness test bed already green-on-paper: 4 `RETAIL` sources → 4 `ANALYTICS_STG`
   views → 2 `ANALYTICS` dynamic-table marts.
3. **A `LineageProvider` seam for catalog pull** — mirrors `OrchestrationProvider`:
   `get_lineage(asset, depth) → graph`. **Marquez is the reference impl**
   (`GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:{ns}:{name}` — purpose-built, Apache-2.0,
   2 containers on the compose stack; slow release tagging is an accepted risk).
   **DataHub** = same seam, deferred until a user brings one (8 GB-RAM footprint
   disqualifies it from the reference stack; our slice-1 emitter already feeds its
   native OL receiver). **OpenMetadata: do not integrate via SDK** — its
   `openmetadata-ingestion` package is Collate source-available (non-compete), which
   ADR 0031 prohibits; REST-only if ever, behind its own ADR. **Purview parked**
   (Atlas API, preview-grade OL, Azure-only — contra the wind-down).

Blast radius = walk `lineage_edges` downstream from the failing asset, join back to
assets that have suites/checks. Slice 2 alone answers it for dbt-modeled warehouses;
slice 3 widens the sources without changing the query.

## 3. Incident objects — stateful, deduped, evidence-carrying

An **alert** is a per-result notification (fire-and-forget, already shipped: severity
routing, dedup, snooze). An **incident** is the stateful object those signals roll up
into:

- **Lifecycle:** `open → acknowledged → resolved` (+ `resolved_by: user | auto`).
  Timestamps and actor per transition. Reopen = new incident linked to the old one.
- **Dedup vs alerts:** at most **one open incident per `(asset_id, check_id)`**;
  repeat failures while open attach as occurrences (`occurrence_count`,
  `last_seen_at`) instead of new incidents — reusing the alerting dedup key
  discipline (#386's shared severity rank), one level up. Alerts keep firing per
  their own dedup/snooze rules and reference the open incident.
- **Auto-resolve on pass** (first passing result for the pair), on by default,
  per-suite configurable — manual ack/resolve always wins over auto.
- **Payload = the Theme-2 deterministic evidence card, layer 1, no LLM:** upstream
  pipeline run (status + delay vs. history, via `triggered_by` correlation),
  `metric_value` trend (sudden vs. drift), profile-diff of failing vs. last-passing
  batch, sibling checks on the same asset, and downstream blast radius from §2.
  Snapshotted as JSONB at open, refreshed per occurrence; delivered on the existing
  `ResultPublisher` seam so the ticket/webhook arrives with the diagnosis attached
  (Theme-5 tier-1 create-only targets get it for free).
- **Ownership routing:** today the incident routes to the **suite owner** (+ the
  suite's notification config). `assets.owner_user_id` is the later hop — once asset
  owners exist, routing prefers asset owner, falls back to suite owner. No new
  routing engine; it's a recipient-resolution function.

## 4. Authz & surfaces

- **Asset visibility = derived, not granted.** An asset is visible iff the caller can
  `view` ≥1 suite mapped to it; the asset page aggregates **only** the
  suites/results the caller's grants cover (ADR 0027 ladder untouched, 404-no-leak
  for assets wholly outside one's grants). Same rule for incidents (visible via the
  incident's suite). Workspace-admins see all (0027); Viewers cap at `view` (0033).
- **Asset metadata mutations** (assign owner, description): **workspace-Admin-only**
  at first — the cheap, safe row on the 0033 matrix; widen to composing-suite `edit`
  later if it chafes.
- **UI phasing:** (2) read-only Assets list + asset page (health across suites) →
  (3) lineage panel + incident list on the asset page → (4) navigation inversion
  (sidebar leads with Assets; suites demote to "execution groups"). Phase 4 is where
  DataQ stops feeling like GX-as-a-service — and it is deliberately last.

## Filed phase-1 issues (build order)

1. Asset entity + resolution migration + backfill (the prerequisite of everything).
2. OpenLineage emission from `run_service` (assertions + metrics facets, dark by default).
3. dbt `manifest.json` → `lineage_edges` (parser + cache + blast-radius query).
4. Read-only asset view (API + UI, grant-derived authz).
5. Incident objects (lifecycle, dedup, evidence-card payload, routing).
6. `LineageProvider` seam + Marquez reference impl (compose stack + docs).

1→2/3 are independent after 1; 4 needs 1 (richer with 3); 5 needs 1 (richer with 3+4);
6 extends 3. The dbt/ADLS test bed outlives Azure — `upload_artifacts.py` already
writes the same layout to `file://`, so the local-first posture (#591) keeps the whole
lineage path testable after the wind-down.

## Guiding principle

Assets are what users **reason about**; suites remain how checks **execute** —
split the noun from the verb, don't replace one with the other. Identity comes from
the open spec, lineage comes from systems that already know it, and the incident is
just the durable, deduped, evidence-carrying view of signals DataQ already produces.
Anything that requires authoring a graph, re-keying authz, or a heavyweight catalog
in the reference stack is out.

1	# ADR 0033 — Workspace roles: Admin / Member / Viewer RBAC on the two-axis model
2	
3	- **Status:** Accepted
4	- **Date:** 2026-07-09
5	- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
6	- **Amends:** ADR [0027](0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) — the workspace-admin *source* moves from the `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` env allowlist to a stored `users.role` (this stales 0027's "/me-layer resolved from the allowlist" implementation line and its Related ADR-0010 "off a config allowlist" pointer, not just the top-level framing); the implicit-suite-admin rule is unchanged; the grant model gains one rule (no `edit` shares to Viewers). ADR [0032](0032-email-otp-signin.md) — its OTP signup contract gains `AUTH_OTP_DEFAULT_ROLE`, and its Decision-6 trust statement widens: mailbox compromise of **any stored-role admin** (not only allowlisted addresses) is admin compromise. The inline amendment blockquotes landed in 0027/0032 with ratification (2026-07-09).
7	- **Related:** ADR [0026](0026-auth-api-keys-and-principal-seam.md) (richer-principals direction; PAT-inherits-user preserved), [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md) (change-audit lineage for role mutations — #310), [0010](0010-provider-agnostic-infrastructure-seams.md) (generic identity attributes only — roles are DataQ-stored, no IdP claims read)
8	- **Issue:** umbrella [#744](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/744) → slices #740 (role model) · #741 (enforcement) · #742 (management) · #743 (frontend). Ratified 2026-07-09 — slices unblocked.
9	
10	## Context
11	
12	Authorization today is two axes with one axis degenerate. The fine axis works: every suite-scoped endpoint (REST and MCP identically) gates through `require_permission` on the `view < edit < admin < owner` ladder (ADR 0027). The coarse axis is a binary env allowlist: `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` makes you workspace-admin; everyone else is an undifferentiated "user". Consequences recorded as gap **G-e** ("config-allowlist admin, one validated IdP — fine internally, not sellable"): admins are managed by env-edit + restart; there is no read-only participant tier; and the largest unscoped hole — **connections are workspace-global**, so any authenticated user (even one holding only `view` shares) can delete or re-credential the Snowflake connection every suite runs on (`connection_service` has no ownership gate; `created_by` is display-only).
13	
14	What this ADR is *not*: it does not touch the per-suite ladder, build groups/teams/custom roles, or add per-connection ACLs. The suite ladder remains the fine-grained axis; this ADR formalizes the coarse one.
15	
16	## Decision
17	
18	1. **Three stored workspace roles — `admin | member | viewer` — as a `users.role` column** (server-default `member`, CHECK-constrained; one additive migration, no roles table). Named **Viewer, not Guest**: `AZURE_ALLOW_GUEST_USERS` already means Entra B2B guests, and two "guest" concepts would collide in config and docs.
19	2. **The two axes compose; neither replaces the other.** Workspace role says what *kind* of user you are; per-suite grants say what you can *touch*. A Member with no share on a suite still cannot see it (404 existence-hiding unchanged). Workspace-admin remains implicit `admin` on every suite exactly per ADR 0027 — only its source moves to `users.role`.
20	3. **Capability matrix (the normative table):**
21	
22	| Capability | Admin | Member | Viewer |
23	|---|---|---|---|
24	| See/use suites shared to them | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (view only) |
25	| Create/import suites (become owner) | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
26	| Receive `edit` shares | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ — capped at `view` |
27	| Connections: mutate (create/edit/delete/re-auth) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
28	| Connections: list/reference in suites | ✅ | ✅ | list only |
29	| Connections: `test` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
30	| Mint PATs (token inherits the user, ADR 0026) | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
31	| `/admin` endpoints, implicit suite-admin, workspace-wide visibility | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
32	| Manage roles in-app | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
33	
34	4. **Connection mutations become Admin-only** — the load-bearing row. Connections are shared infrastructure holding credentials; Members consume them, Admins manage them. This is a **breaking change for Members** who managed connections (promote them before upgrading — release-notes obligation in #741). Credentials stay unreadable through every tier; per-connection ACLs/ownership stay deferred and can layer on later without conflicting with this gate.
35	5. **Viewer is capped belt-and-braces**: granting `edit` to a Viewer is rejected at grant time, AND `effective_permission` caps a Viewer's resolved level at `view` (covers legacy rows and demote-after-grant). Suite creation requires Member+.
36	6. **`WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` demotes to bootstrap + break-glass.** On user upsert, an allowlisted email resolves to `admin` (write-through), so existing deployments upgrade with zero config change; the env path also recovers a workspace whose last admin left. `is_workspace_admin()` = stored role OR allowlist.
37	7. **In-app management with a last-admin guard.** `PATCH /admin/users/{id}/role` (admin-gated). The guard's counting rule: **a change must leave ≥ 1 *stored-role* admin; allowlist-resolved admins never satisfy the invariant** — the env path is the recovery mechanism, not the invariant, so the guard can't be talked out of protection by an env entry that may later disappear. Every role change emits a structured audit log line (actor, target, old→new); the durable *table* for change events is ADR [0020](0020-history-and-audit-strategy.md)'s deferred cross-entity audit log (#310 — a mutation event, not G-d/#431's data-access *read* audit). Roles resolve per request, so a change — including for the target's PATs, which are their user — takes effect on their next request with no session machinery.
38	8. **ADR 0032 interplay (amends 0032)**: OTP signups get `AUTH_OTP_DEFAULT_ROLE` (default `member`; set `viewer` for cautious domain-wide allowlists) — delivered with the role-model slice #740, activating when the OTP slices land. **Precedence: the allowlist write-through wins over any signup default** — an operator on both the OTP signup allowlist and `WORKSPACE_ADMIN_EMAILS` gets stored `admin` at first sign-in, never `member`-stored-but-admin-effective, so dropping the env entry later can't silently demote the bootstrap admin the last-admin guard was counting.
39	
40	## Consequences
41	
42	**Positive** — G-e's "config-allowlist admin" objection is answered with one column and two small gates; the connection-deletion hole closes; a safe read-only tier exists for stakeholders; enforcement stays in the existing seams (`require_permission`, a new `require_role` mirroring `require_workspace_admin`), so REST/MCP can't drift — with MCP parity for each **new** gate (suite-create, share-grant validation, connection mutations) an explicit test obligation in #741, not an assumption. Groups, custom roles, and per-connection ACLs layer on later without schema rewrite; when ADR 0026 phase 2 generalizes users → principals, **the role attribute moves with that migration** (service-account principals carry an explicit role at creation) — this ADR deliberately does not pre-decide the principal schema.
43	
44	**Negative / accepted** — Members lose connection-write (breaking; migration note + promote-first guidance in #741); a fixed three-role enum won't satisfy enterprises wanting custom roles (deliberately deferred); role changes are audit-*logged* but not yet audit-*tabled* (#310); the allowlist break-glass means an env-level actor can always mint an admin — unchanged from today, recorded here **and carried into `docs/security.md` / `docs/compliance-posture.md` by #742** (the ADR alone is not where operators/auditors read the admin model).
45	
46	## Alternatives considered
47	
48	- **Custom roles / permission-matrix tables** — rejected for now: single-tenant, small-team product; a fixed enum covers the sellability gap and the matrix can be introduced later behind the same `require_role` seam.
49	- **External policy engine (OPA / Casbin / SpiceDB)** — rejected: heavy operational dependency for three roles and one resource ladder; nothing in the current model needs relationship-graph evaluation.
50	- **Folding the suite ladder into roles** (role decides everything) — rejected: loses per-resource control; a Member must not see unshared suites.
51	- **Per-connection ACLs now** — rejected: Admin-only mutation closes the actual hole with one rule; ownership semantics (who may grant, what happens on owner deletion) deserve their own decision when multi-team demand is real.
52	- **"Guest" naming** — rejected: collides with Entra B2B guest terminology already present in config (`AZURE_ALLOW_GUEST_USERS`).
53	- **Keep the env allowlist as the only admin source** — rejected: that *is* G-e; but it survives as bootstrap/break-glass rather than being removed.
54

# ADR 0034 — Asset entity with OpenLineage identity; lineage is emitted/pulled, never built

- **Status:** Accepted
- **Date:** 2026-07-10
- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
- **Related:** ADR [0012](0012-check-kind-severity-and-metric-value.md) (`metric_value` feeds the metrics facet), [0027](0027-suite-permission-model-workspace-admin.md) / [0033](0033-workspace-roles-rbac.md) (asset authz derives from the suite ladder; asset-metadata mutation is an Admin capability row), [0029](0029-dbt-orchestration-provider.md) (the artifact reader the manifest parser extends), [0031](0031-oss-byol-distribution-licensing.md) (rules out the OpenMetadata SDK)
- **Issue:** [#596](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/596) (gap G-d design). Full design: [docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md](../post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md).

## Context

Gap G-d: DataQ has runs and alerts but no answer to "what broke downstream, who owns it, when was it resolved." Closing it needs lineage, incidents, an asset page, and (later) governance-catalog sync — and all four need the same missing primitive: today "the table" exists only implicitly inside `Suite.target` JSONB, so there is nothing for lineage edges, incidents, or catalog entities to reference. Separately, a lineage *source* already exists unconsumed: the ADR-0029 dbt provider polls `run_results.json`, and the sibling `manifest.json` (the model dependency graph) lands at the same artifacts URI every build.

## Decision

1. **A first-class `assets` table is the shared primitive, shipped first and alone.** Suites resolve their target to an `asset_id` on save; runs stamp it at dispatch; a backfill migration resolves existing targets. Suites remain the execution/authz grain (ADR 0027 untouched) — assets are the browse/reason grain. Two axes, like dbt models-vs-jobs.
2. **Asset identity = the OpenLineage dataset naming spec (`namespace` + `name`), adopted verbatim as the canonical key** — including its normalization rules (quote-strip, engine-returned case, the OL Snowflake account normalization) so our identifiers match `openlineage-dbt`/Spark emissions byte-for-byte, making future emission/pull interop a join instead of a mapping layer. Consequences accepted with it: DEV/QA accounts are *distinct* assets (cross-env grouping is a DataQ UI concern over the asset's `env` column, never an identity merge), and a flat-file pattern's asset is its literal base prefix (the Spark convention).
3. **Lineage is emitted and pulled, never authored.** Three slices, in order:
   - **Emit OpenLineage** from `run_service` via `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0): START/COMPLETE/FAIL RunEvents with the target asset as input dataset carrying `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` (+ metrics facet for `metric_value` kinds). Dark by default (console transport, `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored); one emitter feeds Marquez/DataHub/Kafka with zero per-catalog code.
   - **Parse dbt `manifest.json`** (fetched by the ADR-0029 3-scheme artifact reader) into a **`lineage_edges` cache** (`upstream_asset_id`, `downstream_asset_id`, `source`, `last_seen`) — a refreshed cache of external truth, not a graph we construct. Minimal stable field subset only (`parent_map`/`child_map` + node identity; never `compiled_code`), version-gated on `metadata.dbt_schema_version` (v12, stable dbt-core 1.8→1.11), ephemeral models collapsed, stream-parsed. This is the zero-infra blast-radius floor.
   - **A `LineageProvider` seam** (mirrors `OrchestrationProvider`) for catalog pull, **Marquez as the reference impl** (purpose-built `GET /lineage` API, Apache-2.0, 2 containers on the compose stack; stalled release tagging accepted as low-risk for a dev-time reference consumer).
4. **Incident objects anchor to assets**: at most one open incident per `(asset_id, check_id)`, lifecycle `open → acknowledged → resolved`, occurrences instead of duplicates, auto-resolve-on-pass (per-suite configurable), the Theme-2 deterministic evidence card as payload, routed to the suite owner today / asset owner later. Alerts remain per-result notifications that reference the open incident.
5. **Asset/incident visibility is derived from suite grants, never separately granted** — visible iff the caller can `view` ≥1 composing suite, aggregation filtered to their grants, 404-no-leak preserved. Asset-metadata mutation (owner, description) starts workspace-Admin-only per the 0033 matrix pattern.

## Consequences

**Positive** — one additive migration unblocks four features (lineage, incidents, asset page, catalog sync); identity interop with the OL ecosystem is free forever; the dbt slice needs no new infrastructure and outlives Azure (`file://` artifacts); the emitter makes DataQ a good OL citizen before asking anything of the ecosystem; no authz re-keying, no graph engine to own.

**Negative / accepted** — OL naming makes cross-environment "same logical table" a two-asset reality the UI must group over; `lineage_edges` freshness is bounded by the artifact-poll cadence; Marquez's release cadence is slow; blast radius is only as complete as the sources feeding the cache (dbt first — warehouse-internal lineage like raw ADF copies won't appear until a catalog/OL source covers it); asset rows accrete as targets change (`last_seen` + a sweep, not deletes, is the cleanup posture).

## Alternatives considered

- **Build our own lineage graph** (the original v1-roadmap "React Flow, ~1 week" sketch) — rejected: authoring and maintaining graph truth is a product in itself; every serious catalog already knows it; pull/emit is cheaper and neutral (Theme 14).
- **Internal surrogate identity for assets** (own ID scheme, map to OL names later) — rejected: the mapping layer is permanent tax, and the OL spec already solves the hard cases (accounts, three-level names, file paths).
- **Asset-level ACLs** — rejected: re-keying authz off suites is a painful migration for little gain (ADR 0027's rationale); derivation keeps one authz source of truth.
- **DataHub as the reference consumer** — rejected for the compose stack (Kafka + OpenSearch + MySQL, 8 GB RAM minimum); it still works via the same emitter/seam when a user brings one.
- **OpenMetadata via its Python SDK** — rejected outright: `openmetadata-ingestion` is Collate source-available with a non-compete clause, prohibited by ADR 0031; REST-API-only integration would need its own ADR.
- **Purview / Atlas now** — parked: preview-grade OL support, Azure-only hosting, contra the wind-down posture.

input.command:
gh issue create --title 'feat(assets): asset entity — `assets` table (OpenLineage identity) + target resolution + backfill (G-d phase 1)' --label enhancement --label priority/P1 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'Phase 1 of the G-d build (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §1, ADR 0034; filed by #596). **The one migration everything else rides — ships first and alone.**

- `assets` table: `id, namespace, name, env, connection_id (FK, nullable — provenance hint, not identity), owner_user_id (nullable, routing hop for incidents), first_seen, last_seen`; unique `(namespace, name)`.
- **Identity = OpenLineage dataset naming, adopted verbatim** (ADR 0034 decision 2): per-connection-type namespace/name templates from the resolver table in the design doc; quote-strip; engine-returned case; OL Snowflake account normalization (hyphenated org-account passthrough, legacy-locator region/cloud defaults) so identifiers match `openlineage-dbt`/Spark byte-for-byte. Flat-file `pattern` targets resolve to the pattern base prefix.
- Resolver: `resolve_target()` output + connection config → `(namespace, name)`; suites resolve to `asset_id` on save; runs stamp `asset_id` at dispatch (history never rewrites on target change).
- Backfill migration resolves every existing `Suite.target` (backward-compatible, additive only — CLAUDE.md migration rules).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Additive migration (assets + suite/run linkage), up+down tested locally
- [ ] Resolver unit-tested per connection type incl. normalization edge cases (quoted identifiers, legacy Snowflake locators, UC 3-level, flat-file patterns) + adversarial inputs
- [ ] Backfill resolves all seeded/demo suites; dispatch stamps `asset_id` on new runs
- [ ] DEV/QA same-table produces two assets (documented, tested — grouping is a UI concern over `env`)' 2>&1 | tail -1

input.command:
gh issue create --title 'feat(lineage): OpenLineage emission from run_service — RunEvents + DataQualityAssertions/metrics facets, dark by default' --label enhancement --label priority/P2 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'G-d build slice (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §2.1, ADR 0034 decision 3a; filed by #596). Depends on #757 (asset identity supplies the dataset namespace/name).

- `openlineage-python` (Apache-2.0, license-checked) emit hooks in `run_service`: START on dispatch, COMPLETE/FAIL on finish; job namespace `dataq`, job name from the suite; the target asset as input `Dataset`.
- `DataQualityAssertionsDatasetFacet` per check result (check → `assertion`, outcome → `success`, severity tier → `severity`, observed/expected → `actual`/`expected`); `DataQualityMetricsInputDatasetFacet` for `metric_value` monitor kinds (freshness/volume — ADR 0012).
- **Dark by default:** no transport configured → no emission; `OPENLINEAGE_DISABLED` honored; standard `OPENLINEAGE__TRANSPORT__*` env config; failures to emit never fail the run (fail-open, logged).
- One emitter reaches Marquez/DataHub (native OL HTTP receiver)/Kafka with zero per-catalog code — the vendor-neutrality argument (ADR 0010/0013).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] START/COMPLETE/FAIL events with correct dataset naming (matches #757 resolver output byte-for-byte)
- [ ] Assertions + metrics facets populated from real result rows; PII discipline: no sample rows in events
- [ ] Off-by-default verified (no config → zero network calls); emit failure cannot fail or slow a run (fail-open test)
- [ ] Verified end-to-end against a local Marquez (compose) receiving events' 2>&1 | tail -1; gh issue create --title 'feat(lineage): dbt manifest.json → `lineage_edges` cache + blast-radius query (zero-infra floor)' --label enhancement --label priority/P2 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'G-d build slice (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §2.2, ADR 0034 decision 3b; filed by #596). Depends on #757. Extends the ADR-0029 dbt provider — the 3-scheme artifact reader (`file`/`adls`/`s3`) fetches the sibling `manifest.json` already landing at `<artifacts_uri>/latest/` unconsumed.

- Parse the **minimal stable subset only**: `metadata.dbt_schema_version` (gate on v12; warn+best-effort below), `parent_map`/`child_map`, per-node `database`/`schema`/`alias`/`relation_name`/`resource_type`/`config.materialized`. Never load `compiled_code`/`raw_code` (manifests reach tens of MB) — stream-parse.
- Table-level edges: models/seeds/snapshots + sources only; tests/operations dropped; **ephemeral models collapsed** (null `relation_name` → recurse to nearest physical ancestors).
- Map nodes → assets via the #757 resolver (dbt-ol-compatible naming makes this a join); upsert into `lineage_edges(upstream_asset_id, downstream_asset_id, source, last_seen)` — a refreshed cache of external truth, not an authored graph. Refresh on the existing 10-min poll beat.
- Blast-radius query: walk downstream edges from a failing asset, join to assets with suites/checks.
- Test bed: the harness dbt project (4 `RETAIL` sources → 4 `ANALYTICS_STG` views → 2 `ANALYTICS` marts) — its real `manifest.json` as a fixture; `file://` keeps this testable post-Azure-wind-down.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Parser fixture-tested against the real harness manifest (v12) + adversarial battery (truncated/NUL/oversized/missing keys per tests/support)
- [ ] Ephemeral collapse + test/operation filtering unit-tested
- [ ] Edges land as asset-id pairs; re-poll upserts (no duplicates); removed models age out via `last_seen`
- [ ] Blast-radius query returns the expected downstream set for a mart-source failure on the fixture graph' 2>&1 | tail -1

input.command:
gh issue create --title 'feat(assets): read-only asset view — /assets API + Assets page (grant-derived authz)' --label enhancement --label priority/P2 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'G-d build phase 2 (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §4, ADR 0034 decision 5; filed by #596). Depends on #757; richer with #759 (lineage panel arrives with #759 data).

- `GET /api/v1/assets` + `GET /assets/{id}`: aggregation across suites targeting the asset — checks, latest results, freshness/volume state, trends.
- **Authz derived, never granted:** asset visible iff caller can `view` ≥1 composing suite; aggregation filtered to the caller''s grants; 404-no-leak for assets wholly outside them (ADR 0027 discipline); workspace-admin sees all; Viewer capped per ADR 0033. Asset-metadata mutation (owner/description) workspace-Admin-only.
- Frontend: Assets list + asset detail page (health across suites); sidebar nav addition — **no navigation inversion yet** (that is phase 4, deliberately last).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Authz matrix tested: owner / edit-share / view-share / no-share / workspace-admin / Viewer — incl. partial-grant aggregation filtering and 404-no-leak
- [ ] Asset page renders health across ≥2 suites targeting the same asset (seeded demo data)
- [ ] MCP parity decision recorded (asset read tool or explicit defer)
- [ ] ≥80% coverage on new modules (W8 gate)' 2>&1 | tail -1; gh issue create --title 'feat(incidents): incident objects — lifecycle, dedup vs alerts, evidence-card payload, ownership routing' --label enhancement --label priority/P2 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'G-d build phase 3 (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §3, ADR 0034 decision 4; filed by #596). Depends on #757; evidence card richer with #759 (blast radius) + the Theme-2 layer-1 dossier task.

- `incidents` table anchored to `(asset_id, check_id)`: lifecycle `open → acknowledged → resolved` (+`resolved_by: user|auto`, actor + timestamp per transition; reopen = new incident linked to prior).
- **Dedup:** at most one open incident per (asset, check); repeat failures attach as occurrences (`occurrence_count`, `last_seen_at`). Alerts stay per-result notifications (existing dedup/snooze untouched) and reference the open incident.
- **Auto-resolve on pass** (first passing result for the pair), default on, per-suite configurable; manual transitions always win.
- **Payload = Theme-2 deterministic evidence card (layer 1, no LLM):** upstream pipeline run + delay-vs-history, `metric_value` trend, profile-diff failing-vs-last-passing, sibling checks, downstream blast radius. JSONB snapshot at open, refreshed per occurrence; delivered via the existing `ResultPublisher` seam.
- **Routing:** suite owner + suite notification config today; prefer `assets.owner_user_id` when set (fallback suite owner). Recipient-resolution function, no new routing engine.
- Visibility derives from suite grants (same rule as #760).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Lifecycle + dedup state machine unit-tested (open/ack/resolve/auto-resolve/reopen; occurrence attach; no duplicate opens under concurrent failing results — upsert race discipline per #420)
- [ ] Evidence card assembled from existing data only, PII-redacted, snapshotted + refreshed
- [ ] Publisher payload carries the card; per-suite auto-resolve config honored
- [ ] Authz: incident visibility matrix mirrors asset rules' 2>&1 | tail -1; gh issue create --title 'feat(lineage): `LineageProvider` seam + Marquez reference impl (catalog pull)' --label enhancement --label priority/P3 --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --body 'G-d build slice (design: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` §2.3, ADR 0034 decision 3c; filed by #596). Depends on #757 + #759 (extends the edge cache with catalog-pulled sources); pairs with #758 (the emitter feeds Marquez; this pulls the graph back).

- `LineageProvider` abstraction mirroring `OrchestrationProvider` (ADR 0004/0011 discipline — no provider-specific branching in service code): `get_lineage(asset, depth) → graph`.
- **Marquez reference impl:** `GET /api/v1/lineage?nodeId=dataset:{ns}:{name}&depth=N`; identity join is free because #757 adopted OL naming. Pulled edges upsert into `lineage_edges` with `source=marquez`.
- Marquez added to the reference docker-compose (2 containers, reuses the Postgres pattern); stalled release cadence (0.50.0, 2024-10) recorded as accepted risk (ADR 0034).
- **Deferred behind the same seam** (do not build): DataHub (footprint), OpenMetadata (SDK license-blocked per ADR 0031 — REST-only would need its own ADR), Purview (Azure-only, parked).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Seam + Marquez impl with contract tests (mocked HTTP) + adversarial payload battery
- [ ] Compose stack: emitter (#758) → Marquez → pull-back round-trip green locally
- [ ] Pulled edges merge with dbt-sourced edges without duplication (source-scoped upsert)
- [ ] ADR 0034 "Related" updated if scope shifts' 2>&1 | tail -1

result.output:
### Test-management publishing (TestRail, Xray, Zephyr)

DQ artifacts map cleanly: suites → test plans, checks → cases, runs → test runs with results.
A result-*exporter* seam (a `ResultPublisher` sibling — per-run push, not per-alert) publishes
run outcomes into the test-management tool of record; pairs with Theme 9's PDF export (#345)
as the "reporting into someone else's system of record" family.

### Data-governance catalogs (+ OpenLineage)

Two directions, one seam:

- **Pull** — consume the catalog's glossary/PII classifications as an authoritative
  classification source (feeds Theme 4's privacy pack + #433, ranked above profiler
  heuristics), and **pull lineage** to power the RCA blast-radius (Theme 2) and gap G-d
  instead of building a lineage graph ourselves.
- **Push** — publish DQ results as **quality facets/assertions on catalog entities**, so data
  consumers see check status where they shop for data. DataHub and OpenMetadata both have
  first-class assertion/data-quality APIs — natural open-source reference impls; Microsoft
  Purview covers the Azure story; Collibra / Atlan / Alation are commercial targets behind the
  same seam.
- **Open standard first: emit OpenLineage events** from runs — one vendor-neutral event stream
  consumed by Marquez, DataHub, Purview, Atlan and the orchestrators' own lineage backends;
  cheaper and more neutral than any point integration, and the likely first slice.

**Shared primitive:** this whole section keys on the **asset entity** (Theme 3's Asset-first IA
capture, phase 1) with **OpenLineage dataset naming as the canonical identity** — build that
first; catalog mapping, lineage nodes, and quality-facet publishing all resolve through it.

### Observability & deploy portability

- **ADR 0034** (+ index row) — the load-bearing decisions: asset identity = the OpenLineage dataset-naming spec adopted **verbatim** (dbt-ol/Spark byte-compatible normalization; DEV/QA = distinct assets by design); **lineage emitted/pulled, never built** (OL emission w/ `DataQualityAssertions`/metrics facets → dbt `manifest.json` → `lineage_edges` cache → `LineageProvider` seam, Marquez reference; OpenMetadata SDK rejected on ADR-0031 license grounds — Collate source-available non-compete); incidents anchor to `(asset_id, check_id)` with the Theme-2 evidence card as payload; asset/incident visibility **derived from suite grants** (ADR 0027/0033 untouched).
- **Phase-1 build issues filed** (`v1.1 Backlog`, v1.2 generator input): #757 asset entity (prerequisite) · #758 OL emission · #759 dbt-manifest lineage · #760 asset view · #761 incidents · #762 LineageProvider/Marquez.
- **Trackers:** roadmap Theme 3/14 + G-d gap row cross-linked; progress.md W3 (+row, 10/13) / W6 (−row, 0/14, exit-gate note) / Backlog paragraph / Snapshot (75 open issues); #596 milestone retargeted W6→W3.

## Research grounding (4 parallel agents, cited in-doc)
OpenLineage naming spec verified against the spec + reference impls; dbt manifest v12 stability verified against actual dbt-core 1.5–1.11 wheels + our harness project'\''s real `manifest.json` (expected graph: 4 `RETAIL` sources → 4 `ANALYTICS_STG` views → 2 `ANALYTICS` marts); catalog comparison (Marquez/DataHub/OpenMetadata/Purview) license-checked per ADR 0031.

- [x] Design doc merged: incident objects (lifecycle, dedup vs alerts), asset-entity anchoring (OpenLineage naming), lineage pull-first slice, ownership routing, RCA-card-as-payload
- [x] Phase-1 issues for the next cycle filed (asset entity migration first) — #757–#762

content.text:
Review target: `763`

`high effort → 3+5 angles × 6 candidates → 1-vote verify (recall-biased) → ≤10 findings`

You are reviewing for **recall** at high effort: catch every real bug a careful
reviewer would catch in one sitting. At this level, catching real bugs matters
more than avoiding false positives. Err on the side of surfacing.

## Phase 0 — Gather the diff

Run `git diff @{upstream}...HEAD` (or `git diff main...HEAD` / `git diff HEAD~1`
if there's no upstream) to get the unified diff under review. If there are
uncommitted changes, or the range diff is empty, also run `git diff HEAD` and
include the working-tree changes in scope — the review often runs before the
commit. If a PR number, branch name, or file path was passed as an argument,
review that target instead. Treat this diff as the review scope.

## Phase 1 — Find candidates (3 correctness angles + 3 cleanup angles + 1 altitude angle + 1 conventions angle, up to 6 each)

Run **8 independent finder angles** via the Agent tool. Each
surfaces **up to 6 candidate findings** with `file`, `line`, a one-line
`summary`, and a concrete `failure_scenario`.

### Angle A — line-by-line diff scan

Read every hunk in the diff, line by line. Then Read the enclosing function for
each hunk — bugs in unchanged lines of a touched function are in scope (the PR
re-exposes or fails to fix them). For every line ask: what input, state, timing,
or platform makes this line wrong? Look for inverted/wrong conditions,
off-by-one, null/undefined deref, missing `await`, falsy-zero checks,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in catch, unescaped regex metachars.

### Angle B — removed-behavior auditor

For every line the diff DELETES or replaces, name the invariant or behavior it
enforced, then search the new code for where that invariant is re-established.
If you can't find it, that's a candidate: a removed guard, a dropped error
path, a narrowed validation, a deleted test that was covering a real case.

### Angle C — cross-file tracer

For each function the diff changes, find its callers (Grep for the symbol) and
check whether the change breaks any call site: a new precondition, a changed
return shape, a new exception, a timing/ordering dependency. Also check callees:
does a parallel change in the same PR make a call unsafe?

### Reuse

The angles above hunt for bugs; this one and the next two hunt for cleanup in
the changed code. Flag new code that re-implements something the codebase
already has — Grep shared/utility modules and files adjacent to the change,
and name the existing helper to call instead.

### Simplification

Flag unnecessary complexity the diff adds: redundant or derivable state,
copy-paste with slight variation, deep nesting, dead code left behind. Name
the simpler form that does the same job.

### Efficiency

Flag wasted work the diff introduces: redundant computation or repeated I/O,
independent operations run sequentially, blocking work added to startup or
hot paths. Also flag long-lived objects built from closures or captured
environments — they keep the entire enclosing scope alive for the object's
lifetime (a memory leak when that scope holds large values); prefer a
class/struct that copies only the fields it needs. Name the cheaper
alternative.

### Altitude

Check that each change is implemented at the right depth, not as a fragile
bandaid. Special cases layered on shared infrastructure are a sign the fix
isn't deep enough — prefer generalizing the underlying mechanism over adding
special cases.

### Conventions (CLAUDE.md)

Find the CLAUDE.md files that govern the changed code: the user-level
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, the repo-root CLAUDE.md, plus any CLAUDE.md or
CLAUDE.local.md in a directory that is an ancestor of a changed file (a
directory's CLAUDE.md only applies to files at or below it). Read each one
that exists, then check the diff for clear violations of the rules they state.

Only flag a violation when you can quote the exact rule and the exact line
that breaks it — no style preferences, no vague "spirit of the doc"
inferences. In the finding, name the CLAUDE.md path and quote the rule so the
report can cite it. If no CLAUDE.md applies, return nothing for this angle.

Cleanup, altitude, and conventions candidates use the same
`file`/`line`/`summary` shape; in `failure_scenario`, state the concrete
cost (what is duplicated, wasted, harder to maintain, or which CLAUDE.md rule
is broken) instead of a crash. Correctness bugs always outrank cleanup,
altitude, and conventions findings when the output cap forces a cut.

Pass every candidate with a nameable failure scenario through — finders that
silently drop half-believed candidates bypass the verify step and are the
dominant cause of misses.

## Phase 2 — Verify (1-vote, recall-biased)

Dedup near-duplicates (same defect, same location, same reason → keep one). For
each remaining candidate, run **one verifier** via the Agent tool:
give it the diff, the relevant file(s), and the candidate; it returns exactly
one of **CONFIRMED / PLAUSIBLE / REFUTED**.

**PLAUSIBLE by default** — do not refute a candidate for being "speculative" or
"depends on runtime state" when the state is realistic: concurrency races,
nil/undefined on a rare-but-reachable path (error handler, cold cache, missing
optional field), falsy-zero treated as missing, off-by-one on a boundary the
code does not exclude, retry storms / partial failures, regex/allowlist that
lost an anchor. These are PLAUSIBLE.

**REFUTED** only when constructible from the code: factually wrong (quote the
actual line); provably impossible (type/constant/invariant — show it); already
handled in this diff (cite the guard); or pure style with no observable effect.

Keep **CONFIRMED and PLAUSIBLE**. Drop REFUTED.

## Output

Return findings as a JSON array of at most 10 objects:

```json
[
  {
    "file": "path/to/file.ext",
    "line": 123,
    "summary": "one-sentence statement of the bug",
    "failure_scenario": "concrete inputs/state → wrong output/crash"
  }
]
```

Ranked most-severe first. If more than 10 survive, keep the 10 most
severe. If nothing survives verification, return `[]`.


## Posting to GitHub (--comment)

The `--comment` flag was passed. After producing the findings list, if the
review target is a GitHub PR, post each finding as an inline PR comment via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` (one call per finding;
include a suggestion block only when it fully fixes the issue). If that tool
is not available in this session, fall back to `gh api` (repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr}/comments)
or print the findings instead. If the target is not a PR, print the findings
to the terminal and note that `--comment` was ignored.

The DataQ doc's line 30 (`unity_catalog` → namespace `unitycatalog://{workspace_host}`, name `catalog.schema.table`) matches the current canonical OpenLineage naming spec exactly. Fetching the live spec at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/main/website/docs/spec/naming.md shows a dedicated "Unity Catalog" row under the Catalog type category specifying `Namespace: unitycatalog://{host}` (or `unitycatalog://{host}:{port}`) with name format `{catalog}.{schema}.{table}` — this is distinct from the separate "Databricks File System (DBFS)" row (`dbfs://{workspace name}`), which the candidate finding wrongly claims is the only Databricks entry. This is corroborated by the reference Spark integration source at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenLineage/OpenLineage/main/integration/spark/spark40/src/main/java/io/openlineage/spark40/agent/lifecycle/plan/catalog/UnityCatalogHandler.java, whose `unityCatalogNamespace()` method literally constructs `"unitycatalog://" + authority`, with an inline comment explaining the scheme/path are dropped "to maintain consistency with the other warehouse namespaces in the OpenLineage naming spec." The candidate finding's premise — that the spec documents only `dbfs://{workspace}` for Databricks and that `unitycatalog://` is an invented scheme — is factually wrong on both the spec table and the reference implementation.</result>

---

**G-d (assets + lineage + incidents) design shipped 2026-07-10** — #596 pulled forward W6→W3, merged as PR #763: `docs/post-v1-assets-lineage-incidents-notes.md` + ADR 0034.

Load-bearing decisions (ADR 0034):
- **Asset identity = OpenLineage dataset naming, verbatim** (namespace+name; `unitycatalog://{host}` IS a real spec row — a review finder wrongly disputed it). DEV/QA = distinct assets by design; UI groups over `env`. Flat-file pattern asset = base prefix.
- **Lineage emitted/pulled, never built:** OL emission from run_service (dark by default) → dbt `manifest.json` → `lineage_edges` cache → `LineageProvider` seam (Marquez reference). **OpenMetadata SDK is license-blocked** (Collate source-available non-compete, ADR 0031); DataHub deferred (footprint); Purview parked.
- Incidents: one open per `(asset_id, check_id)`, reopen = NEW linked incident, evidence card payload, `ResultPublisher` delivery, suite-owner→asset-owner routing. Asset/incident authz derived from suite grants (no asset ACLs).

Build order (v1.1 Backlog, v1.2 generator input): #757 asset entity (prerequisite) → #758 OL emission · #759 dbt-manifest lineage → #760 asset view · #761 incidents · #762 LineageProvider/Marquez. Nav inversion files after #760.

Test-bed facts (verified against the real artifact): harness dbt project `dataq_lineage` = 4 `RETAIL` sources → 4 `ANALYTICS_STG` views → 2 `ANALYTICS` dynamic-table marts; manifest schema **v12 stable dbt-core 1.8→1.11**; artifacts at ADLS `raw/dbt/latest/` AND `file://`-capable (`upload_artifacts.py`), so the lineage path survives the Azure wind-down [[project-azure-winddown]]. Parse only `parent_map`/`child_map` + node identity, never `compiled_code`/`raw_code`; collapse ephemeral (null `relation_name`).

Related: [[project-v11-w3-state]], #310 (audit ADR, still W6, pairs with this).
